topic: SOTA object detection for small aerial objects / anti-UAV / drone-vs-bird (2024-2026)
summary: As of mid-2026, DETR-family transformers and well-tooled CNN detectors are external research candidates for small aerial objects. RF-DETR's code and Nano-through-Large tiers are Apache-2.0, while XL/2XL use PML-1.0. SAHI slicing, a stride-4 P2 head, and transformer prediction heads are complementary techniques, not an inventory of implemented Manwe training features. Manwe currently implements SAHI sliced inference only; slicing-aided training and P2 heads are planned. Every performance, placement, licensing, and dataset claim must be rechecked for the exact artifact and protocol.

items.name: RF-DETR (Nano/Small/Medium/Large/2XL)
items.whatItIs: Roboflow's real-time detection transformer (ICLR 2026) uses a pre-trained DINOv2 ViT backbone plus weight-sharing neural architecture search to trace an accuracy-latency Pareto curve per target dataset; it supports detection and instance segmentation. RF-DETR-N reaches 48.0 AP on COCO, RF-DETR-M 54.7 AP at 4.52 ms T4, and RF-DETR-2XL 60.1 AP. The code and Nano-through-Large tiers are Apache-2.0; XL/2XL use PML-1.0.
items.whyForManwe: An external fine-tuning candidate, not a Manwe or crebain adapter. Only the code and Nano-through-Large tiers are Apache-2.0; XL/2XL use PML-1.0, and every checkpoint/data license still needs review. Verify graph conversion, tensor semantics, placement, and fidelity on the exact artifact.

items.name: Ultralytics YOLO26
items.whatItIs: Publicly released in January 2026 and documented in a June 2026 paper, this unified real-time family has native NMS-free end-to-end inference via a dual one-to-one/one-to-many head, removes DFL, and adds MuSGD, Progressive Loss, and STAL. It spans 40.9-57.5 mAP on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT; the end-to-end branch trails the NMS variant by 0.6-0.8 AP.
items.whyForManwe: Best-tooled CNN track for manwe's dual Apple/NVIDIA training: trains out-of-the-box on both MPS and CUDA and has the most mature one-command export to CoreML (.mlpackage/.mlmodelc), ONNX and TensorRT of any option, with proven ANE deployment. NMS-free output simplifies the export graph. Licensing caveat: AGPL-3.0, a commercial-use consideration for crebain.

items.name: YOLOv11 / YOLOv12
items.whatItIs: Mature Ultralytics detectors. YOLOv11 is the current production workhorse; YOLOv12 (NeurIPS 2025) is attention-centric (Area Attention + FlashAttention + R-ELAN), with YOLOv12-N at 40.6 mAP/1.64 ms T4 and YOLOv12-X at 55.2 mAP. Both use the same Ultralytics train/export toolchain.
items.whyForManwe: YOLOv11 has a substantial Apple deployment ecosystem, but Manwe's Rust graph and Python export paths have different artifact and tensor contracts. YOLOv11/v12 are research candidates, not drop-in upgrades; each backend needs a pinned artifact, preprocessing fixture, tensor inspection, and fidelity gate. Same AGPL-3.0 caveat.

items.name: D-FINE
items.whatItIs: DETR variant (ICLR 2025 Spotlight, Apache-2.0) that reformulates box regression as Fine-grained Distribution Refinement. D-FINE-L reaches ~54.0 AP and D-FINE-X ~55.8 AP on COCO; the ecosystem supports export to ONNX, TensorRT, OpenVINO, CoreML and LiteRT.
items.whyForManwe: Apache-2.0 transformer with a genuinely multi-backend export story (covers all of manwe's targets except MLX) and reported strong fine-tuning on the drone-captured VisDrone benchmark (higher IoU/F1 than YOLO26 in some settings). A good Apache alternative if RF-DETR's DINOv2 backbone is too heavy for the ANE.

items.name: DEIM (Dense O2O + Matchability-Aware Loss)
items.whatItIs: CVPR 2025 training framework (Apache-2.0) that accelerates DETR convergence via Dense one-to-one matching plus a Matchability-Aware Loss. Drop-in on RT-DETR/D-FINE: DEIM-D-FINE-X hits 56.5 AP at 78 FPS T4 and DEIM-D-FINE-L 54.7 AP at 124 FPS while cutting training time ~50%.
items.whyForManwe: An external training-framework candidate whose convergence gains must be reproduced on the selected detector and data. Manwe does not integrate DEIM or a local-checkpoint transformer fine-tuning loop.

items.name: RT-DETRv4
items.whatItIs: Latest RT-DETR (Oct 2025) that distills semantic knowledge from Vision Foundation Models during training (Deep Semantic Injector + Gradient-guided Adaptive Modulation) with zero added inference cost. RT-DETRv4-L reaches 55.4 AP at 124 FPS and RT-DETRv4-X 57.0 AP at 78 FPS on T4, beating DEIM-X (56.5) and YOLOv13-L.
items.whyForManwe: Highest-AP Apache-lineage real-time transformer for the accuracy track; shares RT-DETR export tooling (ONNX/TensorRT). Useful as the CUDA-side high-accuracy reference against which lighter Apple-deployed models are calibrated.

items.name: RT-DETRv2
items.whatItIs: CVPR-2024-lineage real-time DETR (Apache-2.0) improved with a bag-of-freebies: RT-DETRv2-S 47.9 AP and -M 49.9 AP at 217/161 FPS T4, no speed cost vs RT-DETR.
items.whyForManwe: An external Apache baseline worth evaluating. Related model families do not make weights, graphs, or export paths interchangeable; Manwe would need a dedicated adapter and fixtures.

items.name: SAHI (Slicing Aided Hyper Inference)
items.whatItIs: Open-source framework that tiles high-res images into overlapping patches for both inference and slicing-aided fine-tuning so small objects occupy more pixels. Reported +5.1 to +6.8 AP at inference on VisDrone/xView (FCOS/VFNet/TOOD), up to +12.7 to +14.5 AP cumulatively with slicing-aided fine-tuning. Detector-agnostic (YOLO, MMDetection, HF, Detectron2).
items.whyForManwe: A high-leverage detector-agnostic candidate for recovering recall on tiny/far targets. Evaluate sliced inference, slicing-aided training, and architecture changes as separate and combined ablations rather than assuming one dominates.

items.name: P2 high-resolution detection head (stride-4)
items.whatItIs: Adds a fourth detection layer at 1/4 input scale (160x160 for 640 input) so objects as small as 4x4-8x8 px keep a usable 2x2 feature window instead of collapsing to one point at P3. Used in 2024-2026 drone YOLO variants (PPM-YOLOv11, LAF-YOLOv10, P2-YOLOv8n) for multi-point mAP gains; costs extra params/compute.
items.whyForManwe: Direct architectural fix for the 'drone is a handful of pixels' problem in ground-to-air detection; pairs with SAHI. Add a P2 head to the fine-tuned YOLO/DETR backbone when targeting long-range small-UAS.

items.name: TPH-YOLO (Transformer Prediction Heads)
items.whatItIs: Replaces YOLO prediction heads with self-attention transformer heads, adds an extra high-res head and CBAM attention. On the VisDrone challenge it reaches ~39.4 AP, matching the top entry, and beats prior SOTA on DET-test-challenge.
items.whyForManwe: Proven recipe for drone-captured scenes with scale variation and motion blur, the exact conditions of aerial/counter-UAS imagery. A template for augmenting manwe's detectors with attention heads for the small-object regime.

items.name: QueryDet
items.whatItIs: Cascaded sparse-query mechanism (CVPR 2022 Oral): predicts coarse small-object locations on low-res features then sparsely evaluates high-res features only there. +1.0 AP / +2.0 AP-small on COCO with ~3x faster high-res inference; SOTA-level on VisDrone with 2.3x speedup.
items.whyForManwe: Lets manwe exploit P2-style high-resolution feature maps for tiny drones without paying the full dense-compute cost, important for real-time on-device (Metal/ANE) budgets.

items.name: YOLOE (Real-Time Seeing Anything)
items.whatItIs: ICCV 2025 open-vocabulary detector/segmenter from THU-MIG that keeps YOLO inference speed while supporting text, visual and prompt-free modes (RepRTA, SAVPE, LRPC). +3.5 AP on LVIS over YOLO-Worldv2 with 3x less training and 1.4x faster inference.
items.whyForManwe: Useful for crebain's open-set 'unknown' class and for auto-labeling new drone footage by prompt ('small quadcopter', 'fixed-wing UAV') without retraining, then distilling into the closed-set detector. Runs in the Ultralytics stack so it inherits CoreML/MPS/CUDA support.

items.name: YOLO-World
items.whatItIs: Tencent CNN-based open-vocabulary detector (CVPR 2024) using a prompt-then-detect / offline-vocabulary design; ~20x faster and 5x smaller than Grounding DINO at comparable accuracy, 35.4 AP on LVIS at 52 FPS on V100. V2.1 (Feb 2025) adds image-prompt support.
items.whyForManwe: Fast auto-annotation engine to bootstrap drone/bird/aircraft labels on unlabeled RGB video before fine-tuning a closed-set detector; CNN core is more ANE-friendly than DETR-based open-vocab models.

items.name: Grounding DINO (1.5 Pro)
items.whatItIs: Transformer open-vocabulary/grounded detector; the 1.5 Pro tier reports ~54.3 AP zero-shot on COCO. High accuracy but heavy and high-latency versus YOLO-World/YOLOE (RF-DETR-2XL beats GroundingDINO-tiny by 1.2 AP on RF100-VL while running 20x faster).
items.whyForManwe: Best-quality auto-labeler for building the initial drone/bird/aircraft/helicopter dataset offline; not a deployment detector. Use it to mine hard examples and seed labels, then train the real-time model.

items.name: Anti-UAV410
items.whatItIs: Thermal-infrared UAV tracking benchmark: 410 sequences with ~438K manually annotated boxes, extending the original Anti-UAV set to wild scenes; paired with the SiamDT tracker baseline. Part of a family including Anti-UAV (318 seq RGB+IR), Anti-UAV600 (600 seq IR) and CST Anti-UAV.
items.whyForManwe: The canonical thermal-IR benchmark for the counter-UAS detector; directly feeds crebain's thermal channel and a night-time drone-vs-background training signal that RGB sets lack.

items.name: Drone-vs-Bird (WOSDETC Grand Challenge)
items.whatItIs: The reference RGB benchmark for drone-vs-bird discrimination: 77 sequences (static and moving cameras, 720x576 up to 4K), 8 drone types (3 fixed-wing, 5 rotary), with birds flying in the foreground as distractors. Runs as a recurring grand challenge (ICASSP 2023, IJCNN 2025).
items.whyForManwe: Directly trains crebain's drone-vs-bird class boundary and is the standard for measuring false-positive suppression against birds, a core requirement of the taxonomy. Use it for hard-negative bird mining.

items.name: VisDrone
items.whatItIs: Large drone-captured aerial detection/tracking benchmark that is the de-facto standard for small-object detection research (used to validate SAHI, TPH-YOLO, QueryDet, D-FINE fine-tuning). Dense small objects with heavy scale variation.
items.whyForManwe: Best general small-object aerial benchmark to pre-train/validate manwe's small-object pipeline (P2 head + SAHI) before specializing on drone-specific sets; widely reported so results are comparable.

items.name: ARD-MAV (air-to-air MAV detection)
items.whatItIs: ~107.5K high-res RGB frames (1920x1080, 30 FPS, 60 sequences) from flying/mobile cameras for drone-to-drone detection, with realistic motion blur, scale variation and cluttered backgrounds. Bridges static ground datasets to dynamic aerial scenarios; used by methods like YOLOMG/TransVisDrone.
items.whyForManwe: Trains the air-to-air / moving-camera regime and tiny-target-with-motion-blur cases that ground-based sets miss, relevant if crebain sensors are drone-mounted or track fast crossing targets.

items.name: MMAUD (and Halmstad multi-sensor drone dataset)
items.whatItIs: MMAUD (2024): a multi-modal anti-UAV dataset (~50 seq, ~45K frames) combining RGB + audio + radar + LiDAR against modern miniature drones. The Halmstad multi-sensor set adds synchronized RGB + IR + audio(+radar) collected at airports. (No authoritative public 'Halcyon' set was found; Halmstad is the closest documented match.)
items.whyForManwe: The most relevant benchmarks for crebain's sensor-fusion stack: time-synchronized visual/thermal/acoustic/radar/LiDAR streams needed to develop and evaluate the KF/EKF/UKF/PF/IMM fusion and the acoustic azimuth/elevation branch, not just vision.

keyClaims.claim: RF-DETR-2XL is the first real-time detector to surpass 60 AP on COCO (60.1 AP50:95); RF-DETR-M reaches 54.7 AP at 4.52 ms on an NVIDIA T4, and RF-DETR-N (48.0 AP) beats D-FINE-N by 5.3 AP at similar latency. RF-DETR is Apache-2.0 and built on a DINOv2 backbone with weight-sharing NAS.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: The reported RF-DETR performance and DINOv2/NAS architecture are supported, but only the code and Nano-through-Large model tiers are Apache-2.0; XL and 2XL, including the 60.1-AP tier, use PML-1.0.

keyClaims.claim: Ultralytics YOLO26 (June 2026) spans 40.9-57.5 mAP on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT, removes DFL, and its native NMS-free end-to-end one-to-one head trails the NMS-based variant by only 0.6-0.8 AP; new training tricks (MuSGD, Progressive Loss, STAL) improve small-object accuracy. Ultralytics YOLO is AGPL-3.0.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: YOLO26 was publicly released in January 2026; its June 2026 paper supports the stated architecture, training, license, AP, and latency figures.

keyClaims.claim: SAHI slicing raises detection AP by ~5.1-6.8% on VisDrone/xView at inference (FCOS/VFNet/TOOD), rising to a cumulative +12.7-14.5% AP when combined with slicing-aided fine-tuning, making input tiling rather than architecture the biggest AP-small lever for aerial detection.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: The reported SAHI gains are supported for the cited experiments, but they do not establish that tiling is universally more important than architecture; slicing and architecture are complementary ablations.

keyClaims.claim: Among Apache-2.0 transformers, RT-DETRv4-L reaches 55.4 AP at 124 FPS and RT-DETRv4-X 57.0 AP at 78 FPS on T4 (exceeding DEIM-X's 56.5 with no added inference cost), while DEIM-D-FINE-X hits 56.5 AP at 78 FPS and DEIM cuts training time ~50%; this cluster leads pure-COCO real-time accuracy in the license-permissive lane.

Apple-Silicon deployment strongly favors CNN YOLO: CoreML export moves YOLO11 onto the Apple Neural Engine (a case study lifted iPhone inference from ~21 to ~85 FPS) and Ultralytics defaults iOS to .cpuAndNeuralEngine; RF-DETR ships a native Swift/CoreML SDK but its DINOv2/deformable-attention core is less ANE-native, and no mainstream small-aerial detector ships MLX training weights.

Core public drone benchmarks span modalities: Anti-UAV410 has 410 thermal-IR sequences with ~438K annotated boxes; Drone-vs-Bird/WOSDETC has 77 RGB sequences (8 drone types) with birds as foreground distractors; and multimodal sets MMAUD (RGB+audio+radar+LiDAR) and Halmstad (RGB+IR+audio+radar) supply synchronized streams for fusion, while no authoritative public 'Halcyon' counter-UAS dataset could be located.

recommendations: Adopt a two-track detector strategy in manwe: (1) an accuracy/fine-tuning track built on RF-DETR (Apache-2.0, DINOv2, strongest domain adaptation on RF100-VL) as the primary drone detector, exported to ONNX + CoreML .mlmodelc + TensorRT; (2) a latency/tooling track on Ultralytics YOLO26 or YOLO11 for the most mature CoreML/ANE + MPS + CUDA path. Keep D-FINE/RT-DETRv4 (both Apache) as CUDA-side high-accuracy references.

Treat the input pipeline as the main AP-small lever: integrate SAHI into both the training pipeline (slicing-aided fine-tuning) and the eval harness (sliced inference), and add a stride-4 P2 high-res head (optionally QueryDet-style sparse queries to control cost) to whichever backbone is deployed. These beat swapping detector architectures for tiny far-range drones.

Build the fine-tuning corpus by modality and map it to crebain's drone/bird/aircraft/helicopter/unknown taxonomy: Anti-UAV410/Anti-UAV600 (thermal), Drone-vs-Bird/WOSDETC (RGB, and mine birds as hard negatives to cut false positives), VisDrone + DOTA (small/oriented aerial), and ARD-MAV (air-to-air, moving camera). Report AP-small explicitly, not just overall AP.

Use open-vocabulary models offline to bootstrap labels and the open-set 'unknown' class: Grounding DINO 1.5 Pro (highest quality) and YOLO-World/YOLOE (fast, ANE-friendlier) to auto-annotate raw footage and mine hard examples, then distill into the closed-set real-time detector rather than deploying the open-vocab model directly.

Harden the export/parity story manwe already targets: add automated conversion + numerical-parity tests across ONNX, CoreML .mlmodelc, MLX safetensors and TensorRT. Explicitly verify ANE vs GPU op placement for transformer detectors (deformable/multi-scale attention frequently falls off the ANE), and treat MLX as an inference-only experiment path for now since MLX lacks mature detection-training ops; train on PyTorch/MPS (Apple) and CUDA (NVIDIA).

Make licensing an explicit gate for crebain: prefer Apache-2.0 models (RF-DETR, D-FINE, DEIM, RT-DETRv2/v4) for anything shipped in the product, and treat AGPL-3.0 Ultralytics YOLO (v8/11/12/26) as a research/benchmarking track or license it commercially before productizing.

Prioritize multimodal datasets (MMAUD, Halmstad multi-sensor) now, since crebain's differentiator is fusion: they provide the time-synchronized RGB/IR/acoustic/radar/LiDAR needed to co-develop the vision detector with the acoustic azimuth/elevation branch and the KF/EKF/UKF/PF/IMM fusion, rather than integrating vision in isolation.

verifications.claim: RF-DETR-2XL is the first real-time detector to surpass 60 AP on COCO (60.1 AP50:95); RF-DETR-M reaches 54.7 AP at 4.52 ms on an NVIDIA T4, and RF-DETR-N (48.0 AP) beats D-FINE-N by 5.3 AP at similar latency. RF-DETR is Apache-2.0 and built on a DINOv2 backbone with weight-sharing NAS.
verifications.rationale:
I verified each component independently against the primary paper (arXiv:2511.09554, "RF-DETR: Neural Architecture Search for Real-Time Detection Transformers," ICLR 2026), the official roboflow/rf-detr GitHub repo and docs, and the Roboflow blog. The performance claims mostly hold; the licensing claim is materially misleading.

SUPPORTED components:
1) 60.1 AP / first real-time >60 AP: The paper abstract states "RF-DETR (2x-large) is the first real-time detector to surpass 60 AP on COCO" and Table 2 lists 60.1 AP50:95 (78.5 AP50). The repo corroborates 60.1 AP at 17.2 ms on T4/TensorRT-FP16 — genuinely real-time. This is the authors' own claim but is internally consistent and plausible.
2) RF-DETR-M = 54.7 AP: Confirmed in the paper's Table 2 and the repo (54.7 AP50:95, 576x576).
3) RF-DETR-N 48.0 AP beating D-FINE-N by 5.3 AP at similar latency: Confirmed in the paper's Table 2 (RF-DETR-N 48.0 AP at 2.3 ms vs D-FINE-N 42.7 AP at 2.1 ms; 48.0-42.7 = 5.3). Note the comparison is latency-matched, not parameter-matched — RF-DETR-N is ~30.5M params vs D-FINE-N ~4M.
4) DINOv2 backbone + weight-sharing NAS: Confirmed. The paper replaces LW-DETR's CAEv2 backbone with DINOv2 and applies end-to-end weight-sharing NAS over resolution, patch size, window-attention blocks, decoder layers, and query tokens.

WEAK / MISLEADING components:
5) "4.52 ms" for RF-DETR-M: This exact figure comes from the Roboflow blog (a cited source), but the peer-reviewed paper's Table 2 reports 4.4 ms for RF-DETR-M on T4. Minor sourcing inconsistency; not a fabrication, but the paper's number is 4.4, not 4.52.
6) "RF-DETR is Apache-2.0": MATERIALLY MISLEADING as a blanket statement. Per the official repo and docs, only the core package/code and the Nano-through-Large models are Apache-2.0. RF-DETR-XL and RF-DETR-2XL (delivered via rfdetr[plus]) are licensed under PML 1.0 — NOT Apache-2.0. This matters because the very model anchoring the headline (2XL at 60.1 AP) is the one that is NOT Apache-2.0. For a counter-UAS deployment where license terms drive what can ship, conflating these is a real error.

Minor: The repo now lists RF-DETR-N at 48.4 AP (updated checkpoints) vs the paper's 48.0; official D-FINE-N is 42.8 AP (vs 42.7 in RF-DETR's table), so the real-world gap is ~5.2-5.6 depending on source — the paper's own numbers give exactly 5.3.

Net: performance numbers are essentially correct (with the 4.52-vs-4.4 ms caveat), but the sweeping "Apache-2.0" characterization is wrong for the flagship 60-AP model, so the overall claim is partially-supported rather than fully supported.

verifications.claim: Ultralytics YOLO26 (June 2026) spans 40.9-57.5 mAP on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT, removes DFL, and its native NMS-free end-to-end one-to-one head trails the NMS-based variant by only 0.6-0.8 AP; new training tricks (MuSGD, Progressive Loss, STAL) improve small-object accuracy. Ultralytics YOLO is AGPL-3.0.
verifications.rationale:
Every technical assertion checks out against independent sources (not just the two cited), and both cited URLs are real and back the claims.

1) mAP/latency "40.9-57.5 mAP at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT": CONFIRMED. Official docs give the per-scale table n/s/m/l/x = 40.9/48.6/53.1/55.0/57.5 mAP at 1.7/2.5/4.7/6.2/11.8 ms (TensorRT FP16, T4). Reproduced verbatim in the official arxiv paper 2606.03748 and in Roboflow's independent write-up. The 40.9-57.5 range is the NMS-based (one-to-many) headline number.

2) "Removes DFL": CONFIRMED everywhere — "DFL-Free Regression," direct regression with reg_max=1. Corroborated by docs, both arxiv papers, LearnOpenCV, Roboflow.

3) "Native NMS-free end-to-end one-to-one head": CONFIRMED — YOLO26 is end-to-end NMS-free by default.

4) "Trails NMS-based variant by only 0.6-0.8 AP": CONFIRMED and precise. Table 7 of arxiv 2606.03748 gives e2e vs NMS gaps of 0.8 (n: 40.1 vs 40.9), 0.8 (s: 47.8 vs 48.6), 0.6 (m: 52.5 vs 53.1), 0.6 (l: 54.4 vs 55.0), 0.6 (x: 56.9 vs 57.5) — exactly a 0.6-0.8 AP range. Official docs independently show the nano 40.1-vs-40.9 (0.8) case.

5) "MuSGD, Progressive Loss, STAL improve small-object accuracy": CONFIRMED. MuSGD = hybrid Muon-SGD optimizer; ProgLoss shifts supervision toward the inference-time head; STAL = Small-Target-Aware Label assignment guaranteeing positive coverage for small objects. Small-object benefit is explicitly stated (STAL's purpose; Ultralytics/Sapkota papers cite gains on small/occluded objects and UAV datasets — directly relevant to crebain's aerial use case).

6) "Ultralytics YOLO is AGPL-3.0": CONFIRMED — AGPL-3.0 with a separate commercial Enterprise license.

Both claimed sources are genuine: docs.ultralytics.com/models/yolo26 is the live official page, and arxiv 2606.03748 ("Ultralytics YOLO26: Unified Real-Time End-to-End Vision Models," submitted 2 June 2026) is a real paper authored by actual Ultralytics staff (Glenn Jocher, Fatih Cagatay Akyon, Jing Qiu, et al.) — not fabricated.

One caveat (why this is a nuance, not a refutation): the "(June 2026)" annotation is the date of the official Ultralytics paper, NOT the model's first release. YOLO26 was announced/previewed at YOLO Vision 2025 in September 2025 (docs page created 25 Sep 2025) and had its public launch press release on 14 January 2026 (Business Wire/Morningstar). So "(June 2026)" is defensible as the paper-publication month but should not be read as the model's debut. This does not affect any of the technical/SOTA facts, all of which are accurate.

verifications.claim: SAHI slicing raises detection AP by ~5.1-6.8% on VisDrone/xView at inference (FCOS/VFNet/TOOD), rising to a cumulative +12.7-14.5% AP when combined with slicing-aided fine-tuning, making input tiling rather than architecture the biggest AP-small lever for aerial detection.
verifications.rationale:
The empirical figures are accurate and correctly attributed. The SAHI paper (Akyon et al., "Slicing Aided Hyper Inference and Fine-tuning for Small Object Detection," ICIP 2022, arXiv:2202.06934) reports in its abstract that slicing-aided inference increases AP by 6.8% (FCOS), 5.1% (VFNet), and 5.3% (TOOD), and that adding slicing-aided fine-tuning yields cumulative increases of 12.7%, 13.4%, and 14.5% AP respectively — exactly the 5.1-6.8% and 12.7-14.5% ranges cited. The full-text tables (ar5iv) corroborate these as absolute AP percentage-point gains (VisDrone: FCOS 25.8->31.0->38.5; VFNet 28.8->33.9->42.2; TOOD 29.4->34.7->~43.5), and the fcakyon/small-object-detection-benchmark repo reproduces them. So the numeric portion of the claim is SUPPORTED and faithfully sourced.

However, the concluding clause — "making input tiling rather than architecture the biggest AP-small lever for aerial detection" — is an unsupported editorial extrapolation. The paper makes no such comparative claim. It explicitly positions SAHI as a generic, architecture-agnostic pipeline that "can be utilized on top of any existing object detector" WITHOUT architectural modification, i.e., as complementary/orthogonal to architecture, not as a lever that beats it. It never benchmarks tiling against architectural innovations or ranks them. Independent 2024-2025 sources (e.g., learnopencv, ridgerun, and follow-on tiling papers) likewise frame slicing and architecture as complementary strategies rather than competing levers, and note tiling's costs (duplicate detections, border artifacts, higher compute). 

Two minor caveats: (a) the abstract attributes the numbers jointly to "VisDrone and xView," but the specific 12.7-14.5 cumulative figures are the VisDrone results; xView showed much larger relative gains (e.g., FCOS 2.2->17.0 AP), so the per-dataset framing is loose though it mirrors the abstract; (b) the exact IoU metric (COCO AP@[.5:.95] vs AP50) is not disambiguated by the sources, but the paper labels the gains simply as "AP." Net: numbers supported and correctly cited; the "biggest lever, rather than architecture" conclusion is an overreach the source does not make — hence partially-supported.

verifications.claim: Among Apache-2.0 transformers, RT-DETRv4-L reaches 55.4 AP at 124 FPS and RT-DETRv4-X 57.0 AP at 78 FPS on T4 (exceeding DEIM-X's 56.5 with no added inference cost), while DEIM-D-FINE-X hits 56.5 AP at 78 FPS and DEIM cuts training time ~50%; this cluster leads pure-COCO real-time accuracy in the license-permissive lane.
verifications.rationale: Every atomic sub-claim was independently verified against primary sources (official code repos and full paper text, not just the cited abstracts). NUMBERS: The RT-DETRv4 paper HTML (arxiv.org/html/2510.25257v1) explicitly states latency is "measured on a single NVIDIA T4 GPU under TensorRT FP16" and reports RT-DETRv4-L 55.4 AP / RT-DETRv4-X 57.0 AP; the official repo (github.com/RT-DETRs/RT-DETRv4) lists 55.4@8.07ms (=124 FPS) and 57.0@12.90ms (=78 FPS). The DEIM repo table independently confirms DEIM-D-FINE-X 56.5 AP@12.89ms (=78 FPS) and DEIM-D-FINE-L 54.7@8.07ms (=124 FPS). The RT-DETRv4 paper explicitly makes the DEIM-X comparison; RT-DETRv4-X latency (12.90ms) is essentially equal to DEIM-X (12.89ms) and the paper notes DEIM-X is actually slightly slower, so "no added inference cost" is fair (even conservative). DEIM's "~50% training-time reduction" is stated verbatim in its abstract (2412.04234). LICENSES: The claim is about model/code licenses, not the arxiv paper licenses (which are CC-BY for RT-DETRv4 and CC-BY-NC-SA for DEIM). Verified: DEIM's LICENSE file is Apache-2.0; D-FINE (the base of DEIM-D-FINE) is Apache-2.0; and the official RT-DETRv4 repo is Apache-2.0 (code released 2025-11-17). One caveat: the arxiv v1 abstract still says code "will be open source very soon," but by mid-2026 it is released under Apache-2.0, so the license claim holds today. EVALUATIVE "LEADS" CLAIM: Holds under its explicit qualifiers. The strongest independent competitor, RF-DETR (Roboflow, ICLR 2026), reaches 60+ AP on COCO but is legitimately excluded on two grounds: it relies on Objects365 pretraining (excluded by "pure-COCO"), and its 60+ AP XL/2XL variants ship under PML-1.0 rather than Apache-2.0 (only Nano–Large are Apache). YOLOv13 is AGPL-3.0 (outside the permissive lane) and lower AP (~53.4 L). Thus, restricted to pure-COCO-trained, real-time, Apache-2.0 transformers, RT-DETRv4-X (57.0) does lead with DEIM-D-FINE-X (56.5) just behind. The only honest hedges: (1) "no added inference cost" and the "leads" framing originate from the RT-DETRv4 authors' own paper (mild self-interest, but the latency figures corroborate them), and (2) the "lead" is real only because of the deliberately narrow "pure-COCO + permissive" lane — outside "pure-COCO," Objects365-pretrained RF-DETR is more accurate. Neither undermines any specific stated figure. Verdict: supported.

topic: SOTA acoustic drone detection, classification, and direction-of-arrival (DOA) with audio-visual fusion (2023-2026)
summary: Acoustic counter-UAS has bifurcated into two mature research lines that manwe should fuse. (1) Sound Event Localization and Detection (SELD), driven by the DCASE challenges on the STARSS23 corpus, is the right formulation for producing azimuth/elevation/range: systems jointly do sound-event detection, DOA regression, and (since DCASE 2024) source-distance estimation. The dominant architecture is a ResNet-Conformer CRNN fed multichannel log-mel plus spatial features (SALSA-Lite inter-channel phase, or FOA intensity vectors); the 2024 winner (Du_NERCSLIP) hit 54.4% F / 13.6 deg DOA audio-only and improved to 11.4 deg with audiovisual fusion. Linear-complexity Mamba/SSM variants (SELD-Mamba) are the emerging trend. (2) Drone-specific audio-visual trajectory work centers on the MMAUD multimodal anti-UAV dataset (mic array + LiDAR + radar + vision with mm-accurate Leica/Leica-tracker ground truth). Here self-supervised audio-visual Mamba models (AV-DTEC: 99.3% class accuracy, 0.67 m position error, only 10 M params) beat the supervised CRNN+ResNet50 cross-attention baseline (AV-FDTI), and audio-only Mamba (TAME) reaches 98% class / 0.55 m. Classification backbones have moved from PANNs CNN14 (0.431 mAP) and AST (0.485) to self-supervised BEATs/EAT (~0.486) and the current polyphonic SOTA SSLAM (0.502 mAP, ICLR 2025) — the polyphonic focus matters because real airspace audio is many overlapping sources. Honest maturity gap: the strongest recent models rely on Mamba selective-scan CUDA kernels with no Metal equivalent, so heavy training needs NVIDIA while Apple Silicon is best used for MPS/CoreML inference and for the CNN/transformer (non-Mamba) branches.

items.name: SELD ResNet-Conformer (Multi-ACCDOA) — DCASE 2024 Task 3 winning line
items.whatItIs: The de-facto SOTA Sound Event Localization and Detection architecture: a ResNet feature extractor + Conformer sequence model (often with Squeeze-and-Excitation) that jointly outputs per-class activity, Cartesian DOA, and (DCASE'24) source distance via a Multi-ACCDOA head. The 2024 winner Du_NERCSLIP scored F 54.4% / DOA 13.6 deg / RDE 0.21 audio-only on STARSS23, improving to 55.8% / 11.4 deg with audiovisual fusion.
items.whyForManwe: This is the cleanest blueprint for producing exactly what crebain needs — azimuth+elevation (DOA) AND range (distance) per detected source — from a mic array, in one network. The ResNet-Conformer is pure conv+attention (no Mamba), so it trains and runs on both CUDA and Apple MPS, and exports to ONNX/CoreML.

items.name: SALSA-Lite spatial feature
items.whatItIs: Spatial Cue-Augmented Log-Spectrogram (Lite): multichannel log-mel/log-linear spectrograms stacked with normalized inter-channel phase differences, exactly aligned in time-frequency. A 30x-faster approximation of SALSA (which uses covariance eigenvectors). Improves localization F1 by ~15% and localization recall by ~5% over the classic log-mel + GCC-PHAT feature set on TAU-NIGENS 2021.
items.whyForManwe: The recommended input feature for a planar/arbitrary (non-ambisonic) drone mic array. Cheap enough for real-time embedded DOA, preserves the phase cues that carry azimuth/elevation, and is the standard front-end for the ResNet-Conformer SELD head. Pure FFT/tensor ops — runs fine on Metal/MPS.

items.name: AV-DTEC (Self-Supervised Audio-Visual Mamba fusion)
items.whatItIs: Audio-Visual Mamba (Temporal + Spectral audio Mamba + Vision Mamba) with residual cross-attention fusion and a teacher-student adaptive mechanism, trained self-supervised on LiDAR-derived (DBSCAN) pseudo-labels. On MMAUD it reaches 99.3% drone-type accuracy and 0.67 m average position error with only 10.16 M params — beating supervised AV-FDTI (94.0% / 1.24 m / 92 M).
items.whyForManwe: Current SOTA for audio-visual drone trajectory+class fusion and directly relevant: it fuses a mic array with a camera to output 3D hemispherical trajectory (=> az/el/range) and drone class, is deployable-small, and uses LiDAR pseudo-labels so crebain can auto-label from its fusion stack. Caveat: Mamba selective-scan needs CUDA kernels (Apple-Silicon training gap).

items.name: AV-FDTI (Audio-Visual Fusion for Drone Threat Identification)
items.whatItIs: Audio-visual drone identifier: CRNN over the mic-array audio + pretrained ResNet50 over an omnidirectional camera image, fused via a visual-information-entropy + cross-attention mechanism, trained on mm-accurate Leica tracking ground truth. Outputs drone class and 3D localization. Open-source code released (author yizhuoyang / DR-NTU).
items.whyForManwe: The reproducible supervised baseline for the exact crebain use case (mic array + camera -> class + 3D position). Non-Mamba (CRNN + ResNet50) so it trains on MPS and CUDA and exports cleanly to CoreML/ONNX — a safe first fusion model to stand up before chasing the Mamba SOTA.

items.name: TAME (Temporal Audio-based Mamba)
items.whatItIs: Audio-only drone trajectory+classification: 4-channel 48 kHz audio -> 0.2 s mel-spectrograms; Temporal-Spectral Mamba branches (TDOA + spectral attenuation) with cross-attention temporal-feature enhancement. On MMAUD: 98.0% class accuracy, 0.55 m average position error (Dx=0.11, Dy=0.30, Dz=0.34 m), robust in low light where vision fails.
items.whyForManwe: Shows a compact 4-mic array alone can yield 3D localization + class at night/occlusion — the acoustic fallback when cameras are blind. Provides the audio-only branch to pair with manwe's YOLO vision. Same Mamba/CUDA caveat for training on Apple Silicon.

items.name: SELD-Mamba
items.whatItIs: Replaces the Conformer blocks in the SELD pipeline with bidirectional Mamba (selective state-space) blocks for linear-time long-context modeling of audio sequences, with a two-stage training that first optimizes SED+DOA then adds distance estimation.
items.whyForManwe: The efficiency-oriented successor to ResNet-Conformer for the az/el/range SELD head; attractive for long-window, low-latency streaming DOA. Flag: depends on Mamba CUDA kernels — no first-class Metal support, so prototype on CUDA and consider a Conformer fallback for Apple deployment.

items.name: SSLAM (Self-Supervised Learning from Audio Mixtures)
items.whatItIs: ICLR 2025 self-supervised audio backbone that pretrains on audio mixtures (polyphonic soundscapes), reaching 0.502 mAP on AudioSet-2M — current SOTA — while also topping polyphonic benchmarks (+up to 9.1% mAP). Successor in spirit to BEATs/EAT. Code on GitHub (ta012/SSLAM).
items.whyForManwe: Best-in-class encoder for the detection/classification branch (drone/bird/aircraft/helicopter/unknown). Its polyphonic-mixture pretraining matches real airspace audio where drone, wind, traffic and birds overlap. Standard transformer -> runs on PyTorch MPS/CUDA and exports to ONNX/CoreML; fine-tune on drone data.

items.name: EAT (Efficient Audio Transformer)
items.whatItIs: Self-supervised ViT-style audio spectrogram model (data2vec-2.0-style bootstrap + Utterance-Frame Objective + large inverse-block masking). ~48.6 mAP on AudioSet-2M, SOTA-level on ESC-50/SPC-2, with ~10-15x faster pretraining than BEATs/Audio-MAE. Public code and weights (cwx-worst-one/EAT).
items.whyForManwe: Cheapest path to a strong pretrained audio encoder you can re-pretrain/fine-tune on drone corpora without a GPU farm. Good accuracy/compute trade for the classification head; portable across CUDA and Apple MPS. Pairs with BEATs (Microsoft) as the other well-supported SSL option.

items.name: AST (Audio Spectrogram Transformer) & PANNs (CNN14)
items.whatItIs: AST: first convolution-free attention-only audio classifier — 0.485 mAP AudioSet, 95.6% ESC-50, 98.1% SpeechCommands-V2; HuggingFace-supported. PANNs CNN14: the log-mel CNN baseline (0.431 mAP AudioSet), light and edge-friendly.
items.whyForManwe: Battle-tested, well-supported (HF Transformers, torchaudio) baselines for the single-channel drone-vs-bird-vs-aircraft classifier. PANNs CNN14 is the small-footprint option for on-array/edge inference on Apple Neural Engine via CoreML; AST is the accuracy baseline. Both run on MPS and CUDA today with no exotic kernels.

items.name: GCC-PHAT / SRP-PHAT / MUSIC (+ Neural-SRP)
items.whatItIs: Classical array-processing DOA/TDOA estimators: GCC-PHAT (pairwise time-delay), SRP-PHAT (steered-response power map over a grid), MUSIC (subspace). Modern work feeds SRP-PHAT/GCC maps into CNN/CRNN or uses trainable NGCC-PHAT, and Neural-SRP/SRP-DNN cascades improve reverberant robustness.
items.whyForManwe: Deterministic, no-training DOA baseline and sanity check for the mic array, plus strong hand-crafted input features and a graceful fallback when the neural DOA is uncertain. Trivially runs anywhere (CPU/Metal). Use GCC-PHAT/SRP-PHAT maps as auxiliary inputs alongside SALSA-Lite.

items.name: Audio Array 3D UAV Trajectory + LiDAR Pseudo-Labeling
items.whatItIs: Neural audio-only 3D trajectory estimator on MMAUD: convolutional kernels sweep horizontally for TDOA and vertically for spectral cues, trained as a student against LiDAR-derived pseudo-labels (no manual trajectory annotation). ~0.48 m average position error in good conditions.
items.whyForManwe: Demonstrates the self-supervised recipe crebain can adopt: use the LiDAR/radar/vision tracks in the fusion stack to auto-label acoustic trajectories, removing the acoustic-annotation bottleneck. Simple CNN core -> Apple-Silicon friendly.

items.name: MMAUD (Multi-Modal Anti-UAV Dataset)
items.whatItIs: ICRA-2024 multimodal anti-UAV dataset: cross-shaped omnidirectional audio array + stereo vision + multiple LiDARs + radar, with high-precision Leica ground-truth 3D trajectories. ~1700 s over ~50 sequences; drones incl. Mavic 2/3, Phantom 4, M30, M300, Avata. Basis of the CVPR 2024 UG2+ Track-5 challenge. Code + project page public (ntu-aris/MMAUD).
items.whyForManwe: The single most relevant training/eval set for crebain's acoustic + audio-visual + sensor-fusion goals — it already couples a mic array with LiDAR/radar/vision and mm-accurate GT, and is what AV-DTEC/TAME/AV-FDTI are benchmarked on. Use it to train fusion + trajectory and to generate LiDAR pseudo-labels.

items.name: STARSS23 (Sony-TAU Realistic Spatial Soundscapes) + DCASE SELD sets
items.whatItIs: Real spatial recordings with spatiotemporal (class + DOA + distance) annotations in FOA (4-ch first-order ambisonics) and MIC (tetrahedral) formats; the official DCASE 2023-2025 SELD Task 3 corpus (audio and audiovisual tracks), plus large synthetic SELD mixes.
items.whyForManwe: The standard to pretrain and validate the az/el/range SELD head before drone-specific fine-tuning, and a fixed leaderboard to benchmark manwe's DOA/distance error against published systems. Establishes the FOA vs tetrahedral array-format choice for the hardware.

items.name: DREGON (DRone EGonoise and localizatiON) + IEEE SPCup 2019
items.whatItIs: 8-channel microphone array embedded in a quadrotor UAV, with annotated source positions and characterized ego-noise, for UAV-embedded sound-source localization (DOA). Basis of the IEEE Signal Processing Cup 2019 search-and-rescue localization competition. Free for academic use.
items.whyForManwe: The canonical benchmark for DOA under severe rotor ego-noise — exactly the failure mode a fielded acoustic node faces. Use for ego-noise-robust DOA training/eval and to stress-test the mic-array front-end. (Modest size/single drone are its known limits.)

items.name: DroneAudioset
items.whatItIs: 23.5 h annotated drone-audio corpus for search-and-rescue: 17 mics (two 8-ch circular arrays above/below the drone + 1 center) at 25/50 cm, two DJI drones (F450/F330), two throttle levels, three rooms, SNRs from -57.2 to -2.5 dB. MIT license, on Hugging Face; benchmarks show all methods collapse below -30 dB SNR.
items.whyForManwe: The newest, largest ego-noise / extreme-low-SNR benchmark — ideal for training denoising front-ends and quantifying the SNR floor of acoustic detection, and its multi-ring array informs array-geometry choices. Complements DREGON with far more acoustic diversity.

items.name: DroneAudioDataset (Al-Emadi)
items.whatItIs: Open dataset of drone-propeller recordings (single-channel), augmented with noise, for binary/multiclass drone detection+identification via CNN/RNN. Companion to 'Audio Based Drone Detection and Identification using Deep Learning'.
items.whyForManwe: Quick-start data for the single-mic drone-presence classifier and for augmenting the drone class in the SED head; small and easy to fold into an ESC-50/AudioSet training mix. Good for the CoreML/edge presence-detector baseline.

items.name: Passive MEMS-array + Random Forest drone localization (Acta Acustica 2026)
items.whatItIs: Ground-based square planar array of 8 INMP441 MEMS mics (15 cm spacing, 48 kHz) with MFCC features and a Random Forest: 91.6% detection accuracy, 6.3 deg mean azimuth error, ~1.2 m median 2D position error, 150 ms latency, effective to ~50-60 m outdoors.
items.whyForManwe: A concrete, low-cost, edge-deployable reference design (cheap MEMS array + classical ML) with honest range/accuracy numbers — a realistic baseline node for a distributed crebain acoustic layer, and an interpretability/compute counterpoint to the deep models. Runs on any CPU including Apple Silicon.

items.name: PyTorch MPS + torchaudio / CoreML (vs Mamba CUDA kernels)
items.whatItIs: The practical toolchain: PyTorch (CUDA + Apple MPS) with torchaudio STFT/mel front-ends runs AST/PANNs/BEATs/EAT/ResNet-Conformer for training and inference and exports to ONNX/CoreML .mlmodelc. Mamba SSMs (SELD-Mamba/TAME/AV-DTEC) rely on selective-scan + causal-conv1d CUDA kernels with no first-class Metal implementation.
items.whyForManwe: Defines manwe's honest cross-platform split: do heavy Mamba training on NVIDIA; use Apple Silicon (MPS/ANE via CoreML) for the conv/transformer branches and for on-device inference. MLX has no mature mic-array/audio-SSL stack yet, so treat MLX as inference/export-only for audio for now.

keyClaims.claim: On the DCASE 2024 SELD (STARSS23) challenge, the winning ResNet-Conformer-family system (Du_NERCSLIP) achieved F-score 54.4% / DOA error 13.6 deg / relative-distance-error 0.21 audio-only, and audiovisual fusion improved it to 55.8% / DOA 11.4 deg — i.e., adding vision reduced angular error, and jointly outputting detection + DOA + distance is a solved formulation for az/el/range.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: The approximate winning-line metrics and audiovisual DOA improvement are supported, but exact per-system attribution needs care and joint detection/DOA/distance is a demonstrated formulation, not a solved deployment problem.

keyClaims.claim: On the MMAUD anti-UAV dataset, self-supervised audio-visual Mamba (AV-DTEC) reaches 99.3% drone-type accuracy and 0.67 m average position error with 10.16 M parameters, outperforming the supervised CRNN+ResNet50 cross-attention baseline AV-FDTI (94.0% / 1.24 m / 92.28 M) — ~9x smaller and more accurate.

Audio-only 3D drone localization is viable from a compact 4-channel array: TAME (Temporal Audio Mamba) achieves 98.0% classification and 0.55 m average position error on MMAUD, and remains reliable in low-light where cameras fail.

AudioSet-2M classification SOTA has advanced from PANNs CNN14 (0.431 mAP) to AST (0.485), to self-supervised BEATs/EAT (~0.486), to SSLAM (0.502 mAP, ICLR 2025); SSLAM's polyphonic-mixture pretraining also yields up to +9.1% mAP on polyphonic benchmarks, which matches overlapping-source airspace audio.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: The cited benchmark progression and SSLAM gain are supported, but its urban/speech polyphonic training data do not establish transfer to drone or airspace audio; that analogy requires target-domain evaluation.

keyClaims.claim: SALSA-Lite (inter-channel-phase spatial feature) improves localization-dependent F1 by ~15% and class-dependent localization recall by ~5% over multichannel log-mel + GCC-PHAT, at ~30x lower compute than the eigenvector-based SALSA — making it the preferred real-time array feature.

A low-cost ground node of 8 INMP441 MEMS mics (15 cm spacing) with MFCC + Random Forest gives 91.6% detection accuracy and 6.3 deg mean azimuth error but only ~50-60 m effective outdoor range; deep methods on DroneAudioset collapse below about -30 dB SNR — quantifying the acoustic layer's short range and ego-noise floor.

recommendations: Make the core acoustic model a SELD head with a Multi-ACCDOA output: per-class activity + Cartesian DOA (gives azimuth & elevation) + distance (gives range). This one formulation natively produces the az/el/range detections crebain needs and aligns with a public leaderboard (DCASE/STARSS23) for validation.

Default backbone: ResNet-Conformer (DCASE-proven, pure conv+attention) so it trains and runs on BOTH CUDA and Apple MPS and exports to ONNX/CoreML. Treat SELD-Mamba/AV-DTEC/TAME as a CUDA-only high-accuracy track, not the Apple-deployment path, until Metal selective-scan kernels exist.

Input features: multichannel log-mel + SALSA-Lite (inter-channel phase) for a planar/arbitrary array, or FOA intensity vectors if you choose an ambisonic mic; add GCC-PHAT/SRP-PHAT maps as auxiliary channels and keep classical SRP-PHAT/MUSIC as a training-free DOA fallback and sanity check.

Classification branch (drone/bird/aircraft/helicopter/unknown): initialize from a self-supervised encoder — SSLAM (polyphonic SOTA) or EAT/BEATs — pretrained on AudioSet, then fine-tune on drone corpora. Ship a small PANNs CNN14 / AST variant to CoreML/ANE for on-node edge inference.

Data plan: pretrain SED on AudioSet+ESC-50; fine-tune and evaluate 3D localization + AV fusion on MMAUD (mic array + LiDAR + radar + vision + Leica GT); harden ego-noise/low-SNR robustness on DREGON and DroneAudioset; use Al-Emadi's DroneAudioDataset for a quick binary presence baseline.

Audio-visual fusion: reproduce AV-FDTI first (CRNN audio + ResNet50 vision + cross-attention — MPS/CUDA-portable), then move to AV-DTEC-style residual cross-attention fusion. Fuse the acoustic DOA/range track with manwe's existing YOLO camera detections; time-sync audio and video and share a common world frame.

Use LiDAR/radar/vision tracks (from the crebain fusion stack) to auto-generate pseudo-labels for the acoustic model (as in AV-DTEC and the LiDAR-pseudo-label trajectory work), eliminating the acoustic-annotation bottleneck for site-specific fine-tuning.

Compute SPL directly (not learned): calibrate each mic's sensitivity and emit calibrated broadband + per-octave-band dB SPL alongside the neural detection payload; feed SPL and DOA/range as observations into crebain's KF/EKF/UKF/IMM sensor-fusion tracker (acoustic gives good bearing, weak/short range — weight covariances accordingly).

Hardware: specify a synchronized, geometry-calibrated array of >=4 mics (8-mic planar or tetrahedral/FOA recommended) with a known baseline for TDOA, and hardware time-sync to the cameras; plan for a distributed layer of cheap MEMS nodes (~50-60 m each) since acoustic range is short.

Cross-platform export path: train on NVIDIA (esp. Mamba models), export the deployed detector/SELD head to ONNX + CoreML .mlmodelc (and MLX safetensors where feasible) for Apple inference; document that MLX currently lacks a mature mic-array/audio-SSL training stack, so keep audio training in PyTorch and use MLX/CoreML for on-device inference only.

verifications.claim: On the DCASE 2024 SELD (STARSS23) challenge, the winning ResNet-Conformer-family system (Du_NERCSLIP) achieved F-score 54.4% / DOA error 13.6 deg / relative-distance-error 0.21 audio-only, and audiovisual fusion improved it to 55.8% / DOA 11.4 deg — i.e., adding vision reduced angular error, and jointly outputting detection + DOA + distance is a solved formulation for az/el/range.
verifications.rationale: See verdict rationale.

verifications.claim: On the MMAUD anti-UAV dataset, self-supervised audio-visual Mamba (AV-DTEC) reaches 99.3% drone-type accuracy and 0.67 m average position error with 10.16 M parameters, outperforming the supervised CRNN+ResNet50 cross-attention baseline AV-FDTI (94.0% / 1.24 m / 92.28 M) — ~9x smaller and more accurate.
verifications.rationale: Every number in the claim is verified, and critically, the baseline figures are corroborated by an INDEPENDENT primary source rather than only the cited paper. Extracting the actual AV-DTEC PDF (arXiv 2412.16928): Table I gives AV-DTEC mean accuracy 99.3% and mean APE 0.67 m (day 0.58 m/99.7%, night 0.75 m/98.9%), and AV-FDTI mean accuracy 94.0% and mean APE 1.24 m (day 0.58 m/99.6%, night 1.89 m/88.3%); Table II gives params 10.16 M (AV-DTEC) vs 92.28 M (AV-FDTI). Independently, AV-FDTI's OWN paper (Journal of Automation and Intelligence 3(2024)144-151, DR-NTU PDF) reports the identical mean accuracy of 94.0% and day/night APEs of 0.58 m and 1.89 m (mean 1.235≈1.24 m) — so the baseline was NOT weakened; AV-DTEC used AV-FDTI's published figures. AV-FDTI's architecture (CRNN audio + pretrained ResNet50 vision + cross-attention/visual-entropy fusion) is confirmed by that paper. Arithmetic: 92.28/10.16=9.08x (~9x smaller ✓), 99.3>94.0 (more accurate ✓), 0.67<1.24 (lower error ✓). CAVEATS that qualify the strength but do not refute the numeric claim: (1) 'self-supervised' is the authors' own label but is really LiDAR-pseudo-label auto-labeling (DBSCAN on LiDAR, 'up to 1-meter' pseudo-labels, plus a Faster R-CNN teacher), i.e. weak/auto supervision that avoids AV-FDTI's survey-grade Leica ground truth, not classic self-supervised representation learning. (2) This is not independent validation: AV-DTEC, AV-FDTI, and the MMAUD dataset all come from the same NTU ARIS lab (shared authors Yang/Yuan/Xie) — a single-lab, single-dataset self-comparison with no third-party reproduction. (3) It is an arXiv v1 preprint (Dec 2024, cs.SD); peer-review/acceptance not confirmed, and the repo link in the paper has a typo (AV-DETC). Both methods report day/night means, so the comparison is apples-to-apples. Because the claim only asserts what the papers report (and does not overstate it as deployment-proven or independently validated), and all figures match two independent sources, the verdict is supported with the stated maturity caveats.

verifications.claim: Audio-only 3D drone localization is viable from a compact 4-channel array: TAME (Temporal Audio Mamba) achieves 98.0% classification and 0.55 m average position error on MMAUD, and remains reliable in low-light where cameras fail.
verifications.rationale:
Every specific factual assertion checks out against the primary source, which I retrieved independently (arXiv HTML full text, not just the abstract) plus the independent MMAUD dataset paper.

NUMBERS (exact match): The paper is real — arXiv:2412.13037, "TAME: Temporal Audio-based Mamba for Enhanced Drone Trajectory Estimation and Classification" (Xiao, Hu, Xu, He), accepted at IEEE ICASSP 2025. Table I reports TAME (audio-only) at APE = 0.55 m and Acc = 98.0%, which the paper states is the mean of day and night. So "0.55 m average position error" and "98.0% classification" are quoted correctly. (The claim's parenthetical "Temporal Audio Mamba" is a slight shortening of the actual name "Temporal Audio-based Mamba" — cosmetic, not substantive.)

AUDIO-ONLY / 4-CHANNEL: TAME takes only the mel-spectrogram of audio as input — genuinely audio-only. Independently confirmed via the MMAUD dataset sources (ntu-aris/MMAUD, arXiv:2402.03706): MMAUD includes a four-channel audio array in a cross-shaped omnidirectional microphone configuration for TDOA — so "compact 4-channel array" is accurate.

LOW-LIGHT / CAMERAS FAIL: Supported by Table I's day/night comparison. At night the visual baseline (DarkNet) collapses to APE ≈ 8.31 m / 63% Acc and the audiovisual-fusion baseline (AV-FDTI) drops to ≈ 1.89 m / 94%, while audio-only TAME holds 0.55 m / 98%. Since audio is light-independent, "remains reliable in low-light where cameras fail" is a fair reading; the paper explicitly states visual models decline at night.

HONEST CAVEATS (why this is a benchmark result, not a settled general fact — though they don't contradict the claim as literally worded): (1) The 0.55 m / 98% figures are on MMAUD V1, the EASIER subset where drones fly mostly below 30 m within a fixed ~7×25×22 m volume; the harder V2/V3 used in the CVPR UG2+ challenge reach ~100 m and are not what these numbers reflect. (2) Results are self-reported by a single ICASSP-2025 paper with no independent reproduction I could find; the word "viable" generalizes a controlled, calibrated-array benchmark to a broader claim. (3) Localization accuracy from a compact acoustic array degrades with range, and long-range performance is not demonstrated by these numbers. These are maturity/generalization caveats, not errors in the cited figures — so the claim as stated is supported.

verifications.claim: AudioSet-2M classification SOTA has advanced from PANNs CNN14 (0.431 mAP) to AST (0.485), to self-supervised BEATs/EAT (~0.486), to SSLAM (0.502 mAP, ICLR 2025); SSLAM's polyphonic-mixture pretraining also yields up to +9.1% mAP on polyphonic benchmarks, which matches overlapping-source airspace audio.
verifications.rationale: Every numeric fact in the trajectory was independently verified against primary sources and is real and correctly attributed. (1) PANNs CNN14 = 0.431 mAP: CONFIRMED (PANNs paper, arxiv 1912.10211); the strongest PANN variant Wavegram-Logmel-CNN is 0.439, but CNN14 specifically is 0.431. (2) AST = 0.485 mAP: CONFIRMED (AST abstract, arxiv 2104.01778, Interspeech 2021) — this is AST's ensemble headline; a single AST is ~0.459. (3) EAT = 0.486 mAP: CONFIRMED (arxiv 2401.03497, exactly 48.6% on AS-2M). BEATs single iter3 is ~0.480 and iter3+ ~0.486, so "~0.486" is a fair approximation; note BEATs' famous 0.506 headline is a 10-model ENSEMBLE, not a single model. (4) SSLAM = 0.502 mAP, ICLR 2025: CONFIRMED (arxiv 2506.12222 abstract reports mAP 50.2, up to 3.9% improvement on AS-2M; accepted at ICLR 2025 per GitHub ta012/SSLAM and ICLR proceedings). SSLAM is the single-model AudioSet-2M-only SOTA; nothing clearly exceeds it single-model as of mid-2026 (DASS also ~50.2), though BEATs' 0.506 ensemble technically exceeds it, so the "SOTA...to SSLAM (0.502)" framing holds only under standard single-model comparison. (5) "+9.1% on polyphonic benchmarks": CONFIRMED (SSLAM abstract: performance improvements of up to 9.1% mAP on polyphonic datasets across linear-eval and fine-tuning). Two caveats keep this from being flawless: (a) the claim mixes AST's ensemble headline with single-model numbers for the rest and glosses BEATs single-vs-ensemble — common leaderboard imprecision, not errors, since each cited number is individually real; (b) the trailing clause "which matches overlapping-source airspace audio" is an interpretive extrapolation by the claim's author, NOT established by the paper — SSLAM's polyphonic benchmarks are general acoustic scenes (URBAN-SED, SPASS, urban/speech/environmental mixtures), never drone or airspace audio; airspace audio is genuinely multi-source so the analogy is reasonable, but no evidence shows the gains transfer to counter-UAS acoustics. On source quality: three cited sources are legitimate primary arxiv papers whose numbers I verified directly; codesota.com/audio/classification is a low-authority aggregator but its figures (0.431/0.485/0.480/0.486/0.502) align with the primaries. Net: SUPPORTED for the empirical SOTA trajectory and the +9.1% figure; treat the "matches airspace audio" clause as an unproven domain-transfer analogy rather than a verified fact.

topic: SOTA multi-camera detection, 3D tracking, calibration and triangulation (2023-2026) for a counter-UAS multi-camera triangulation + tracking module
summary: As of mid-2026, multi-camera 3D perception splits into two families. (1) Learned BEV early-fusion detectors/trackers — MVDet -> MVDeTr -> EarlyBird -> TrackTacular -> BEV-SUSHI — project per-camera CNN features onto a shared ground-plane bird's-eye-view occupancy grid and detect/track there; they are SOTA on WILDTRACK/MultiviewX/AI-City (TrackTacular ~93-96 MODA, BEV-SUSHI HOTA 81.22 on AICity'24 3D MTMC). Critically, they assume targets stand on a known ground plane, so they do NOT natively represent airborne drones at altitude and are trained on pedestrians/vehicles. (2) Classical geometric triangulation — undistort, back-project 2D detections to rays through each calibrated camera, and solve N-view DLT (SVD null-space) or the midpoint least-squares for a 3D point with covariance — is fully 3D, sensor-agnostic, cross-platform (pure linear algebra, no CUDA/MPS), and feeds directly into a KF/EKF/UKF/IMM fusion tracker with Mahalanobis gating and M-of-N lifecycle. For crebain's airborne-target problem the geometric path is the correct backbone; the learned BEV stack is the strongest option only for ground-level near-site targets and is CUDA-heavy (BEVFormer deformable-attention custom ops don't run on Apple MPS). Calibration is solved with Zhang's checkerboard method (intrinsics/distortion), COLMAP/pycolmap bundle adjustment or PnP-from-survey (extrinsics), with GeoCalib/AnyCalib as single-image fallbacks. The geometry contract: P_i = K_i[R_i|t_i]; cross-camera association via epipolar/Sampson gating + ReID; triangulate with RANSAC over camera subsets; propagate covariance as the tracker's measurement noise. Time synchronization across cameras (hardware trigger/PTP) is a hard prerequisite for any triangulation.

items.name: N-view DLT triangulation (SVD null-space)
items.whatItIs: For each calibrated view stack the two cross-product constraints (u*P3 - P1)^T X = 0 and (v*P3 - P2)^T X = 0 from projection matrix P_i = K_i[R_i|t_i]; assemble A X = 0 over all cameras and solve by SVD, taking the smallest singular vector, then dehomogenize. Rows can be confidence-weighted by per-detection score.
items.whyForManwe: The core of a counter-UAS multi-camera module: it fuses 2D drone/aircraft detections from any number of calibrated cameras into a full 3D world point with NO ground-plane assumption, so it works for airborne targets. Pure linear algebra (nalgebra in Rust / numpy) runs identically on Apple Silicon and NVIDIA with no GPU custom ops, unlike the learned BEV stack.

items.name: Midpoint / back-projected-ray least-squares triangulation
items.whatItIs: Each 2D detection defines a ray: center C_i = -R_i^T t_i, direction d_i = normalize(R_i^T K_i^{-1}[u,v,1]^T). The 3D point minimizing summed perpendicular distances to all rays is a closed-form linear solve: (sum_i (I - d_i d_i^T)) X = sum_i (I - d_i d_i^T) C_i. Naturally yields a position covariance from ray geometry.
items.whyForManwe: Gives an interpretable 3D fix plus uncertainty for each triangulated drone track, which becomes the measurement + measurement-noise R fed to crebain's KF/EKF/UKF/IMM fusion tracker. Degenerate/near-parallel-ray geometry (distant targets, small baseline) is detectable from the covariance, letting the tracker down-weight weak fixes.

items.name: BEV-SUSHI
items.whatItIs: Meta Reality Labs MTMC 3D detection + tracking: BEVFormer spatial-cross-attention lifts multi-camera features into a shared BEV, a deformable-DETR head predicts 3D boxes, and a hierarchical SUSHI-style graph neural network associates tracks over windows up to 2000 frames using geometry + multi-view ReID. SOTA HOTA 81.22 on AICity'24 3D MTMC (+9.28 over 2nd), IDF1 95.6 / MOTA 92.6 on WILDTRACK.
items.whyForManwe: The strongest learned end-to-end MTMC-3D pipeline and the reference to benchmark against for GROUND-level near-site targets (people/vehicles around a protected asset). Honesty gap: it is ground-BEV-anchored (not for airborne targets) and BEVFormer/deformable-attention needs CUDA custom ops, so it will not run on Apple MPS without a CUDA box; no public code release noted.

items.name: TrackTacular (Lifting Multi-View Detection and Tracking to the BEV)
items.whatItIs: Teepe et al. (CVPRW 2024). Compares BEV lifting methods (parameter-free Simple-BEV bilinear sampling vs Lift-Splat depth splatting), a CenterNet/CenterPoint-style heatmap+offset+motion head, and temporal BEV concatenation. WILDTRACK MODA 93.2 / MOTA 91.8 / IDF1 95.3; MultiviewX MODA 96.5 / MOTA 92.4 / IDF1 85.6. Official code released.
items.whyForManwe: Best open, reproducible learned ground-target option. Its parameter-free Simple-BEV bilinear lifting is just grid_sample (no custom CUDA kernel), making it the most Apple-MPS-portable of the BEV family — a candidate for the ground/near-site detection path that must also train on Apple Silicon. Still ground-plane; not for airborne targets.

items.name: EarlyBird
items.whatItIs: Teepe et al. (WACVW 2024). Extends MVDet by projecting all camera features to the ground plane for unified BEV detection, then learns per-detection Re-ID embeddings for temporal association — joint detection + tracking in one network. Improves WILDTRACK by +4.6 MOTA and +5.6 IDF1 over prior SOTA; generalizes to WILDTRACK, MultiviewX, Synthehicle.
items.whyForManwe: The clearest, simplest early-fusion BEV tracking baseline and the direct predecessor of TrackTacular/BEV-SUSHI; useful as an architecture reference for how multi-view features + ReID fuse before association. Same ground-plane and CUDA-research-code caveats.

items.name: GeoCalib
items.whatItIs: CVG (ECCV 2024) single-image calibration: predicts intrinsics (focal length) + gravity (roll/pitch) and refines them with a self-calibrating bundle-adjustment layer running classical Gauss-Newton steps. Supports pinhole, simple-radial, radial and simple-divisional (fisheye) models. Apache-2.0 code, CC-BY weights, PyTorch, CPU/GPU.
items.whyForManwe: Fast intrinsic-calibration fallback when a camera can't be checkerboard-calibrated (fixed RTSP/PTZ site cameras, archived footage). PyTorch means it likely runs on Apple MPS for inference (no explicit MPS claim — verify); output K/distortion plugs straight into the triangulation contract.

items.name: Zhang's method (OpenCV calibrateCamera)
items.whatItIs: Planar-checkerboard calibration recovering intrinsics K and lens distortion (radial/tangential) from multiple views of a known planar target; the industry-standard first step, exposed as cv2.calibrateCamera / stereoCalibrate. Undistortion (cv2.undistort) must precede any triangulation.
items.whyForManwe: The ground-truth calibration path for cameras crebain owns and can physically wave a board in front of. Deterministic, cross-platform (no GPU), and the accuracy floor for triangulation quality — sub-pixel intrinsic error dominates 3D localization error at long counter-UAS ranges.

items.name: COLMAP / pycolmap (SfM + bundle adjustment)
items.whatItIs: Structure-from-Motion + multi-view-stereo toolkit with Python bindings; jointly optimizes camera intrinsics, extrinsics and 3D points via Ceres (or the new GPU 'Caspar' backend), with explicit multi-camera rig support. Recovers extrinsics (R,t) for a fixed camera array from overlapping imagery.
items.whyForManwe: The practical way to get the extrinsic R,t of a multi-camera site array without hand-surveying every mount — run SfM/bundle adjustment once at install, export per-camera P_i for the triangulation contract. Rig support keeps the array's relative geometry consistent; re-run to auto-recalibrate after a camera is bumped.

items.name: AnyCalib
items.whatItIs: Model-agnostic single-view camera calibration via on-manifold learning; predicts intrinsics across pinhole, radial and fisheye/wide-FoV models from one image, reporting improved robustness/accuracy over GeoCalib across camera types. Code released.
items.whyForManwe: A newer single-image calibration option covering wide-FoV / fisheye lenses often used for cheap wide-area sky coverage in counter-UAS arrays, where GeoCalib's narrower distortion models are weaker. Use as an intrinsic-init before bundle adjustment.

items.name: AI City Challenge MTMC People Tracking (2024/2025)
items.whatItIs: NVIDIA-run MTMC benchmark on synthetic NVIDIA Omniverse warehouses/retail/hospital scenes — ~1300 cameras, ~3400 identities, 100M+ boxes, 1080p@30fps. Camera calibration matrices AND 3D annotations provided; scored by HOTA on 3D locations with a 2 m threshold (online methods get a 10% bonus). 2024 winner Yachiyo 71.94 HOTA; 2025 added a 3D track.
items.whyForManwe: The most relevant public benchmark for the full detect->associate->triangulate->3D-track pipeline because it evaluates in 3D with provided calibration — the exact contract crebain needs. Top stacks (YOLOX/YOLOv8 + TransReID/OSNet ReID + HRNet foot-keypoints) are a concrete recipe. Still ground targets and synthetic-indoor.

items.name: WILDTRACK
items.whatItIs: EPFL 7-camera synchronized+calibrated real outdoor pedestrian dataset: 1080x1920, 400 frames annotated at 2 fps over a 36x12 m area, ~20 persons/frame, each ground location seen by an average of 3.74 cameras; ~40k boxes / 300+ identities. The de-facto multi-view detection/tracking benchmark.
items.whyForManwe: Standard yardstick for the ground-level multi-camera path and for validating the calibration+triangulation code (provides real intrinsics/extrinsics). Its overlap statistic (3.74 cams/point) is a useful design target for how many crebain cameras must see a target for a reliable 3D fix.

items.name: MultiviewX
items.whatItIs: Synthetic (game-engine) counterpart to WILDTRACK from the MVDet authors: 6 calibrated overlapping cameras, 1080x1920, 400 frames @2 fps over 16x25 m, ~40 persons/frame (2x WILDTRACK density). Ground-plane BEV occupancy annotations.
items.whyForManwe: Shows the synthetic-data pattern crebain should copy: because there is NO public airborne multi-camera triangulation benchmark, you must generate a synthetic aerial multi-camera scene (Omniverse/Blender/AirSim) with known 3D drone trajectories and exact calibration to validate the triangulation + 3D tracker.

items.name: MMPTRACK
items.whatItIs: Large-scale densely-annotated multi-camera multi-person tracking benchmark (ICCV 2021 challenge / WACV 2023): 9.6+ hours, 28 subjects, 5 environments (retail/lobby/industry/cafe/office), 4-6 cameras each, per-frame full-body boxes + IDs with 3D tracking labels via a semi-automatic annotation system.
items.whyForManwe: Occlusion-heavy indoor MTMC data useful for stress-testing cross-camera ReID and data-association under heavy occlusion — the failure mode that most degrades triangulation correspondence. Its auto-annotate-then-verify pipeline is a template for labeling crebain's own multi-cam captures cheaply.

items.name: MVDeTr
items.whatItIs: Multiview Detection with Shadow Transformer + view-coherent data augmentation; long the WILDTRACK detection SOTA (~91.5 MODA). Introduces a transformer that reasons over projection 'shadows' created when features are homography-projected to the ground plane.
items.whyForManwe: Foundational reference for why naive homography projection distorts tall/off-ground objects (the 'shadow' problem) — the same distortion that makes ground-plane BEV wrong for airborne drones, motivating the full-3D triangulation choice. Good detection baseline for the ground path.

items.name: MvCHM (Multiview Detection with Cardboard Human Modeling)
items.whatItIs: Detects per-view, estimates depth of standing-point and head to build thin 'cardboard' human point clouds, and fuses them in 3D — explicitly modeling target HEIGHT so vertical pixels project correctly, unlike flat ground-plane BEV methods. Code at github.com/Jiahao-Ma/MvCHM.
items.whyForManwe: The multi-view method that most directly confronts the off-ground / height problem via ray-tracing + depth rather than a flat homography — conceptually closest to what an airborne-target system needs (reason in 3D volume, not a ground grid). Useful design inspiration for a height/volume-aware variant.

items.name: LOSTU (uncertainty-aware triangulation)
items.whatItIs: Fast, scalable, statistically-grounded N-view triangulation that estimates the 3D point together with its uncertainty, improving robustness over plain linear least-squares under observation error.
items.whyForManwe: Directly upgrades the triangulation core: producing a calibrated position covariance per 3D fix is exactly what crebain's Mahalanobis-gated KF/EKF/UKF/IMM tracker needs as its measurement-noise R, rather than a hand-tuned constant. Improves gating and track quality at long, noisy counter-UAS ranges.

items.name: DepthTrack (Cluster Meets BEV)
items.whatItIs: ICCVW 2025 (AI City) multi-camera multi-target 3D tracking that combines depth/clustering with BEV representation for online 3D MTMC on the AI City 3D track.
items.whyForManwe: Recent (2025) datapoint on the online 3D MTMC frontier and how depth priors + clustering complement BEV; relevant if crebain adds per-camera monocular depth to disambiguate cross-camera association before triangulation.

items.name: Distant Object Localisation from Noisy Segmentation Sequences
items.whatItIs: Pesonen/Solin/Honkavaara: multi-view least-squares triangulation of DISTANT objects from noisy segmentation masks, with temporal filtering, explicitly designed for degraded masks and far targets where appearance is weak. Aerial/remote-sensing context.
items.whyForManwe: The most on-point recent work for crebain's actual regime — small, distant airborne targets where detections/masks are noisy — validating the noisy-triangulation-plus-temporal-filter approach over learned ground-BEV. Directly informs the airborne triangulation + tracking design.

keyClaims.claim: BEV-SUSHI is SOTA on the AI City'24 3D MTMC benchmark with HOTA 81.22 (+9.28 over 2nd place) and reaches IDF1 95.6 / MOTA 92.6 on WILDTRACK, using BEVFormer multi-view lifting + a hierarchical GNN tracker.

TrackTacular reaches MODA 93.2 (WILDTRACK) and 96.5 (MultiviewX) for detection and MOTA 91.8/92.4, IDF1 95.3/85.6 for tracking, and finds parameter-free Simple-BEV bilinear-sampling lifting competitive with learned depth splatting.

All MVDet-lineage BEV multi-view methods (MVDet, MVDeTr, EarlyBird, TrackTacular, BEV-SUSHI) fuse features onto a ground-plane BEV occupancy grid and are trained on pedestrians/vehicles standing on the ground, so they do not natively represent airborne targets at altitude — full-3D triangulation is required for the drone case.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: The reviewed methods are ground-anchored and trained on ground-standing targets, but some use 3D internal representations or output 3D boxes. Airborne use needs an altitude-capable validated path; geometric triangulation is one candidate, not the only possible architecture.

keyClaims.claim: WILDTRACK provides 7 synchronized, calibrated 1080x1920 cameras over a 36x12 m area, 400 frames annotated at 2 fps, ~20 persons/frame, with each ground location covered by an average of 3.74 cameras.

The AI City 2024 MTMC track uses synthetic NVIDIA Omniverse scenes with ~1300 cameras, PROVIDES camera calibration matrices plus 3D annotations, and scores HOTA on 3D locations with a 2 m distance threshold; the winning team (Yachiyo) scored 71.94 HOTA.

GeoCalib (ECCV 2024) estimates single-image intrinsics + gravity through a self-calibrating bundle-adjustment layer using classical Gauss-Newton steps, supports pinhole/simple-radial/radial/fisheye models, and ships Apache-2.0 code with CC-BY weights.

recommendations: Make classical N-view triangulation the backbone of the multi-camera module, not learned BEV. Airborne drone/aircraft targets are not on a ground plane, so the entire MVDet->BEV-SUSHI family (ground-BEV occupancy) is architecturally wrong for the primary counter-UAS case. Implement a triangulation crate/module (Rust nalgebra or numpy) with: DLT via SVD null-space AND midpoint ray least-squares, confidence-weighted rows, RANSAC over camera subsets for outlier rejection, reprojection-error gating, and per-point covariance output. This is cross-platform (no CUDA/MPS custom ops) and satisfies the Apple-Metal + NVIDIA-CUDA dual-target requirement trivially.

Define an explicit geometry contract and store it as versioned JSON/YAML per camera: undistort(pixel) -> normalized ray; P_i = K_i[R_i|t_i]; camera center C_i = -R_i^T t_i; ray dir d_i = normalize(R_i^T K_i^{-1}[u,v,1]^T). Downstream: 2D detection (u,v,score) x N cameras -> cross-camera association -> triangulate -> {X_world (3D), covariance} -> hand to the crebain fusion tracker as a measurement with noise R = covariance. Require synchronized timestamps (hardware trigger or PTP); triangulation is invalid across unsynchronized frames.

Build the calibration pipeline in three tiers: (a) Zhang/OpenCV checkerboard for owned cameras (intrinsics+distortion, the accuracy floor); (b) COLMAP/pycolmap SfM bundle adjustment or PnP-from-surveyed-control-points for the array extrinsics R,t, re-runnable to auto-recalibrate after a bump; (c) GeoCalib or AnyCalib as single-image intrinsic fallback for fixed/PTZ/archived cameras that can't be board-calibrated. Persist covariance on extrinsics and propagate it into triangulation uncertainty.

Solve cross-camera correspondence BEFORE triangulating: gate candidate detection pairs by epipolar/Sampson distance using the fundamental matrix from calibration, then confirm with appearance ReID (OSNet or TransReID embeddings, the AI City winning recipe) so drone vs bird vs aircraft identities stay consistent across views. Wrong correspondences are the dominant triangulation failure mode; log per-pair epipolar residuals for debugging.

For the 3D multi-target tracker, do NOT reinvent it inside the camera module — triangulated {position, covariance} points are just another measurement stream into crebain's existing KF/EKF/UKF/IMM + Mahalanobis-gating + M-of-N-lifecycle fusion engine, alongside acoustic azimuth/elevation/range and radar/RF. Use IMM (constant-velocity + coordinated-turn) for maneuvering drones; feed the triangulation covariance directly as measurement noise (see LOSTU) instead of a hand-tuned constant.

Offer the learned BEV stack as an OPTIONAL ground-target path only, clearly scoped to people/ground-vehicles near a protected site. If added, start with TrackTacular (open code, Simple-BEV parameter-free bilinear lifting = plain grid_sample, the most Apple-MPS-portable) and treat BEV-SUSHI/BEVFormer as CUDA-only (deformable-attention custom ops do not run on Apple MPS). Verify TrackTacular actually trains/infers on MPS before committing; document the gap in the README.

Since NO public airborne multi-camera triangulation benchmark exists, generate a synthetic aerial evaluation set (NVIDIA Omniverse / Blender / AirSim) with multiple calibrated cameras, known 3D drone trajectories, and injected detection noise, mirroring how MultiviewX was built for WILDTRACK. Evaluate the triangulation path with 3D localization RMSE and GOSPA, and the tracking path with HOTA/IDF1/MOTA, reusing WILDTRACK/AI-City only to validate the calibration+correspondence+triangulation code end-to-end on real geometry.

Keep the module framework-light and portable: detection stays in the existing YOLO backends (Rust/Candle/Metal, PyTorch/MPS, CoreML/ANE), and the geometry (calibration, triangulation, association) lives in dependency-free linear algebra so it compiles and runs identically on Apple Silicon and NVIDIA. Export the calibration+track schema in the same ONNX/CoreML/MLX/TensorRT-friendly serialization strategy crebain uses for detectors so the whole pipeline deploys on both platforms.

verifications.claim: BEV-SUSHI is SOTA on the AI City'24 3D MTMC benchmark with HOTA 81.22 (+9.28 over 2nd place) and reaches IDF1 95.6 / MOTA 92.6 on WILDTRACK, using BEVFormer multi-view lifting + a hierarchical GNN tracker.
verifications.rationale: Every component of the claim checks out against the primary source and is independently corroborated. (1) HOTA 81.22 on the AICity'24 test set: confirmed in the paper's Table 2 ("Results on AICity'24 test set"), with DetA 86.94 / AssA 76.19 / LocA 95.67. (2) +9.28 over 2nd place: the paper lists the runner-up at 71.94 HOTA; 81.22 - 71.94 = 9.28. I independently verified via the separate 8th AI City Challenge (2024) summary paper (arxiv 2404.09432) that this 71.94 belongs to team Yachiyo (71.9446), which had the highest RAW HOTA on the actual Track 1 leaderboard — so BEV-SUSHI's baseline is a genuine top challenge entry, not a strawman. (3) WILDTRACK IDF1 95.6 / MOTA 92.6: reported in the paper, correctly. (4) BEVFormer multi-view lifting: confirmed — the paper adapts BEVFormer for MTMC 3D detection (removing camera embeddings, enabling dynamic camera counts). (5) Hierarchical GNN tracker: confirmed — it extends SUSHI (a hierarchical-GNN 2D tracker) to 3D with a global tracking layer. TWO HONEST CAVEATS that do not refute the claim but qualify it: (a) The WILDTRACK 95.6/92.6 figures come from the BEV-SUSHI-dagger variant that ingests external EarlyBird detections; the model's own-detector version scores IDF1 93.4 / MOTA 87.5. The claim's word "reaches" fairly reflects the best reported number, but it is the augmented-input variant. (b) BEV-SUSHI is an NVIDIA post-challenge paper (Dec 2024), not a live 2024-competition participant; the 81.22 is a post-hoc evaluation on the released test set benchmarked against published leaderboard entries. Also, Yachiyo (71.94) was the raw-HOTA leader but was the OFFICIAL competition runner-up because the actual winner (Team79 SJTU-Lenovo) received a 10% online-tracking bonus — so "2nd place" is accurate for raw HOTA under the paper's framing. Net: all numbers, the architecture, and the comparison baseline are real and correctly attributed; only minor variant/context nuances apply.

verifications.claim: TrackTacular reaches MODA 93.2 (WILDTRACK) and 96.5 (MultiviewX) for detection and MOTA 91.8/92.4, IDF1 95.3/85.6 for tracking, and finds parameter-free Simple-BEV bilinear-sampling lifting competitive with learned depth splatting.
verifications.rationale:
I independently retrieved and read the full paper tables (arxiv:2403.12573v1 HTML plus the arxiv PDF, which is the CVPRW 2024 3DMV paper "Lifting Multi-View Detection and Tracking to the Bird's Eye View" by Teepe et al., TU Munich). Every numeric value in the claim matches the paper exactly.

Detection (Table 1, TrackTacular rows): TrackTacular's best Wildtrack MODA is 93.2 (Depth Splatting row; other rows: Persp.Proj 91.8, Bilinear 92.1, Deform.Attn 78.4). Best MultiviewX MODA is 96.5 (Bilinear Sampling row; others: Persp.Proj 95.9, Depth Splat 96.1, Deform.Attn 94.4). So "MODA 93.2 (WILDTRACK) and 96.5 (MultiviewX)" are correct as TrackTacular's best per-dataset detection results.

Tracking (Table 2, TrackTacular Bilinear Sampling rows): Wildtrack IDF1 95.3 / MOTA 91.8; MultiviewX IDF1 85.6 / MOTA 92.4. This matches "MOTA 91.8/92.4, IDF1 95.3/85.6" exactly, and all four values come from the same (Bilinear Sampling) configuration.

Qualitative finding: The paper's own words (Sec 4.4, p.7) state "Overall, the parameter-free lifting methods, bilinear sampling, show competitive results for parameterized depth splatting" — a direct match to the claim's "parameter-free Simple-BEV bilinear-sampling lifting competitive with learned depth splatting." The paper attributes bilinear sampling to Simple-BEV (Harley et al., ref [20]) and describes it as parameter-free.
