Honesty caveats (do not refute the claim, but qualify its framing): (1) The two detection MODA figures come from different lifting configurations — 93.2 (Wildtrack) is Depth Splatting while 96.5 (MultiviewX) is Bilinear Sampling — whereas all four tracking figures are Bilinear Sampling; the claim aggregates best-per-dataset numbers. (2) TrackTacular is NOT the top method on Wildtrack detection MODA: the prior two-stage method MVTT reaches 94.1 MODA (> 93.2), and the paper explicitly concedes "Only the two-stage approach MVTT is better in MODA on Wildtrack." The claim does not assert Wildtrack-MODA supremacy, so this is context, not a contradiction. (3) The "competitive" conclusion holds for the pedestrian datasets; on the harder cross-scene Synthehicle vehicle benchmark the paper states the opposite — "The parameterized depth splatting lifting method outperforms the parameter-free bilinear sampling by a significant margin." The claim's scope is the pedestrian detection/tracking results, where the paper's overall statement supports "competitive."

Verification confidence: I did not blindly trust the cited sources; I extracted the raw Table 1/Table 2 values from the arxiv PDF myself and every one of the six numbers plus the qualitative finding checks out. Cross-check attempts via openaccess.thecvf.com (HTTP 403), paperswithcode (redirected), and huggingface papers (404) failed, but the primary arxiv source is authoritative and unambiguous.

verifications.claim: All MVDet-lineage BEV multi-view methods (MVDet, MVDeTr, EarlyBird, TrackTacular, BEV-SUSHI) fuse features onto a ground-plane BEV occupancy grid and are trained on pedestrians/vehicles standing on the ground, so they do not natively represent airborne targets at altitude — full-3D triangulation is required for the drone case.
verifications.rationale:
The core thrust is correct and independently confirmed, but two specific characterizations are imprecise, so the claim is partially rather than fully supported.

WHAT IS SUPPORTED (verified independently):
1) All five ARE in the multi-view BEV detection lineage, and all are trained/evaluated only on ground-standing targets: MVDet, MVDeTr, EarlyBird on pedestrians (Wildtrack/MultiviewX); TrackTacular on pedestrians + vehicles (Synthehicle); BEV-SUSHI on people (AICity'24/WildTrack). No airborne/aerial data appears in any of them. The "trained on pedestrians/vehicles standing on the ground" sub-claim is fully TRUE.
2) The pure ground-plane framing is exactly right for MVDet ("projecting feature maps onto the ground plane (bird's eye view)... convolutions on the ground plane feature map"), MVDeTr ("projections from multiple views onto a common ground plane"), and EarlyBird ("projecting all views to the ground plane... detection in the BEV").
3) All five are ground-anchored: even the more 3D-aware ones assume objects rest on z=0. BEV-SUSHI explicitly defines the world origin "lying on the ground plane" and associates via the "bottom center point" of the 3D box. The BEV output is indexed by (x,y) ground position, so a drone at altitude over ground point (x,y) is not separably represented — the conclusion "do not natively represent airborne targets at altitude" holds for the published methods.

WHY ONLY PARTIALLY SUPPORTED (nuances the claim overstates):
1) "Fuse features onto a ground-plane BEV occupancy grid" is an oversimplification for 2 of the 5. TrackTacular actually lifts features along predicted depth into a 3D voxel grid with multiple height levels (Lift-Splat-Shoot style) before collapsing to BEV. BEV-SUSHI produces full 3D bounding boxes with height and tracks in 3D world coordinates. Both carry a height/z axis internally — architecturally closer to a 3D occupancy volume than the flat "ground-plane occupancy grid" description implies. The limitation is the ground-rest assumption and ground-standing training data, not an inability of the representation to hold a vertical axis.
2) "Full-3D triangulation is required" overstates exclusivity. Extending the BEV lift to a full 3D voxel occupancy volume (a vertical generalization of what TrackTacular/BEV-SUSHI already do) is an equally valid path to airborne targets; classical multi-view triangulation is one option, not the only one. The papers themselves make no such "triangulation required" statement — it is the claimant's engineering inference.
3) Minor sourcing issue: the claim's third cited URL (arxiv 2207.02013) is "Multiview Detection with Cardboard Human Modeling," not MVDeTr (which is 2108.05888). That source still supports the ground-standing point (it models pedestrians as "cardboard point clouds on the ground"), so it does not undermine the claim, but the citation is mislabeled if intended as the MVDeTr reference.

Net: the load-bearing conclusion — these methods are ground-anchored, trained only on ground-standing targets, and do not natively output a free altitude dimension — is well-supported. The claim loses "fully supported" because it flattens TrackTacular's 3D-voxel and BEV-SUSHI's 3D-box representations into a "ground-plane grid" and presents triangulation as the sole remedy.

verifications.claim: WILDTRACK provides 7 synchronized, calibrated 1080x1920 cameras over a 36x12 m area, 400 frames annotated at 2 fps, ~20 persons/frame, with each ground location covered by an average of 3.74 cameras.
verifications.rationale:
Independently verified, every figure in the claim is accurate for the real WILDTRACK dataset, so the statement is factually supported. Confirmed directly from authoritative pages: (1) 7 statically-positioned cameras, jointly calibrated AND synchronized — EPFL CVLAB page states "highly accurate joint-camera calibration as well as synchronization"; arxiv abstract says "seven-static-camera set-up." (2) Resolution 1920x1080 (the claim's "1080x1920" is the same resolution, transposed) — confirmed by EPFL, the arxiv PDF, and datasetninja. (3) Area 12 x 36 m (claim's "36x12" is the same, transposed) — derivable from the 480x1440 grid at 2.5 cm/cell in the arxiv PDF and stated explicitly as "12 x 36 m²" in the CVPR 2018 paper. (4) 400 annotated frames at 2 fps — confirmed by EPFL and the arxiv PDF. (5) ~20 persons/frame — EPFL states "On average, there are 20 persons on each frame." (6) 3.74 cameras/ground-location — this is the canonical figure from the peer-reviewed CVPR 2018 paper (Chavdarova et al.), which is correct.

One sourcing caveat a rigorous fact-check must flag, though it does not falsify the claim: the specific value "3.74" is NOT found in either of the two cited sources. The EPFL page omits it, and the cited arxiv preprint 1707.09299 explicitly gives a DIFFERENT number — "On average, each position is seen from 3.92 cameras" (3.92, not 3.74; datasetninja mirrors this 3.92). The 3.74 value originates from the separate CVPR 2018 conference paper, so the claim mixes figures across two WILDTRACK papers (2017 preprint says 3.92; 2018 CVPR paper says 3.74). Because 3.74 is a genuine, correct, peer-reviewed WILDTRACK statistic and all other numbers verify against the cited sources, the claim is supported, but the 3.74 should be attributed to the CVPR 2018 paper rather than to the two URLs given (one of which reports 3.92 for that metric). Persons/frame also differs by version (~20 in preprint/EPFL, which the claim uses; 23.8 in CVPR 2018).

topic: SOTA multi-target tracking and multi-sensor fusion, with simulation + evaluation harness (2022-2026), for the manwe/crebain counter-UAS stack
summary: As of mid-2026 the field splits into two mature-but-separate stacks. (1) Model-based Bayesian tracking/fusion — KF/EKF/UKF/PF, IMM for maneuvering targets, classical data association (GNN, JPDA, MHT), and random-finite-set filters that have moved from PHD/CPHD approximations to closed-form labeled/PMBM families (GLMB/LMB, PMBM/TPMBM), with 2024-2026 work adding jump-Markov multiple-model variants (MM-TPMBM). The reference open-source harness here is dstl Stone Soup (v1.9.1, MIT, pure Python), which bundles all of the above plus simulators, Mahalanobis gating, M-of-N initiators, and set-theoretic metrics (OSPA/GOSPA/SIAP) — ideal as an offline golden reference, though it is research-grade with no GPU/Apple-Silicon acceleration. (2) Learned sensor-fusion detectors feeding trackers — camera-radar (CenterFusion, CRN) and lidar-camera (BEVFusion, TransFusion, FUTR3D) BEV fusion on nuScenes, plus transformer end-to-end trackers (MOTR/MOTRv2) that now lead on non-linear DanceTrack motion while tracking-by-detection (ByteTrack, OC-SORT) stays competitive and faster on pedestrian benchmarks. For evaluation, HOTA (via TrackEval) is the primary MOT metric but is not a true mathematical metric; GOSPA/T-GOSPA (Garcia-Fernandez) are the mathematically consistent set metrics with clean localization/missed/false/switch decompositions — the right primary metric for a Bayesian fusion tracker. For crebain specifically, the best fit is Stone Soup as the simulation + evaluation harness mirroring the shipped 6-state constant-velocity tracker (Mahalanobis gating, M-of-N lifecycle), cross-checked against Garcia-Fernandez's MTT for RFS baselines, with the production real-time tracker hand-built in Rust and validated against the Stone Soup reference. Learned radar-camera fusion (CRN/CenterFusion-style) is the detector upgrade path but is CUDA-first with a real Apple-Silicon gap.

items.name: Stone Soup (dstl/Stone-Soup)
items.whatItIs: MIT-licensed, pure-Python framework for developing and testing tracking/state-estimation algorithms. Provides KF/EKF/UKF/particle predictors+updaters, IMM predictor for maneuvering targets, data associators (nearest-neighbour/GNN, PDA, JPDA, MHT-style hypothesisers), GM-PHD and GLMB random-finite-set filters, Mahalanobis distance hypothesisers/gaters, M-of-N initiators (MultiMeasurementInitiator) and covariance/time deleters, ground-truth + multi-target + clutter simulators, and OSPA/GOSPA/SIAP metrics.
items.whyForManwe: Best fit for crebain's fusion simulation + evaluation harness. ConstantVelocity 6-state transition model + DistanceHypothesiser(Mahalanobis) + GNN/JPDA associator + MultiMeasurementInitiator (M-of-N confirm) + deleters mirror the target tracker 1:1, giving a golden reference to diff the Rust production tracker on identical seeded scenarios. Natively supports heterogeneous sensors (bearing-only acoustic, range-doppler radar, pixel/az-el camera). Caveat: research-grade, not real-time, no CUDA/Metal/MPS acceleration.

items.name: TrackEval
items.whatItIs: Reference implementation of MOT metrics (HOTA and DetA/AssA/LocA sub-metrics, CLEAR/MOTA/MOTP, IDF1/IDP/IDR, VACE, Track-mAP, J&F), with loaders for MOTChallenge (MOT15/16/17/20), KITTI, DanceTrack, BDD100K, TAO, MOTS. Depends only on numpy/scipy.
items.whyForManwe: Standard way to score the video/multi-camera tracking side of crebain against MOT-style ground truth. Wire into CI so detector/tracker changes get regression-scored on HOTA/IDF1. Complements set-based GOSPA: TrackEval for identity/association quality, GOSPA for Bayesian localization/cardinality error.

items.name: Garcia-Fernandez MTT (PMBM/PHD/CPHD + trajectory filters + GOSPA/T-GOSPA)
items.whatItIs: MATLAB reference library implementing PMBM, MBM, PMB, PHD, CPHD and trajectory variants (TPMBM/TPMB/TMBM/TMB/TPHD/TCPHD), plus GOSPA (with decomposition) and T-GOSPA (LP implementation) metrics. Accompanies ~27 peer-reviewed RFS-tracking papers. Note: does NOT include GLMB/LMB.
items.whyForManwe: Authoritative cross-check for random-finite-set filter behavior and for the set-based metrics crebain should adopt. Its GOSPA/T-GOSPA math (with a companion Python T-GOSPA release) is the metric spec to port into manwe's eval harness. Use it to validate any Rust/Python RFS upgrade beyond the CV/IMM baseline.

items.name: GOSPA and T-GOSPA (quasi-)metrics
items.whatItIs: Generalized Optimal Sub-Pattern Assignment metric for sets of objects (GOSPA) and sets of trajectories (T-GOSPA). Unlike MOTA/HOTA these are mathematically consistent metrics; they decompose total error into localization error (detected objects), missed targets, false targets, and (T-GOSPA) track switches. 2024-2026 quasi-metric variants allow asymmetric missed/false and asymmetric localization costs.
items.whyForManwe: Correct primary accuracy metric for a Bayesian fusion tracker with M-of-N lifecycle: cleanly separates whether errors come from localization, missed drones, false tracks, or ID switches — exactly the tradeoffs gating and confirmation thresholds control. Python T-GOSPA code exists to drop into the harness.

items.name: HOTA (Higher Order Tracking Accuracy)
items.whatItIs: Unified MOT metric balancing detection, association, and localization, decomposable into DetA/AssA/LocA and finer error types. Now the primary metric on MOT17/MOT20/KITTI/DanceTrack. Not a true mathematical metric (fails identity/triangle-inequality), unlike OSPA/GOSPA.
items.whyForManwe: Standard for reporting the multi-camera visual tracking component and comparing against published trackers. Use HOTA for identity/association scoring alongside GOSPA for set/localization scoring; do not use it as the sole metric for the fusion filter.

items.name: CRN (Camera Radar Net)
items.whatItIs: Camera-radar BEV fusion that lifts perspective image features using radar points and fuses the BEV maps with multi-modal deformable attention. It reported 62.4% NDS / 57.5% mAP and about 20 FPS on nuScenes at publication; its then-first-place ranking has since been surpassed.
items.whyForManwe: Most directly relevant learned-fusion detector for crebain, whose core sensor mix is radar + camera (+ thermal/acoustic), not lidar. CRN's radar-guided BEV lifting is the architecture to emulate for range-aware detections feeding the tracker. CUDA-first with custom BEV ops — expect an Apple-Silicon re-implementation gap for MPS/MLX.

items.name: CenterFusion
items.whatItIs: Center-based radar-camera fusion for 3D detection: associates radar detections to image-based object centers via a frustum method, then fuses to regress depth/velocity/attributes. Established the radar-camera fusion baseline on nuScenes (large NDS gains over camera-only CenterNet).
items.whyForManwe: A simpler, well-understood radar-camera fusion baseline to stand up first before CRN-class BEV fusion. Its frustum association is a good conceptual match for fusing az/el camera detections with radar range-doppler in crebain, and it is lighter to port to Apple Silicon.

items.name: BEVFusion (MIT-HAN-Lab)
items.whatItIs: Unified multi-task multi-sensor BEV fusion for lidar+camera; encodes both modalities into a shared BEV representation with an optimized BEV-pooling op. Reports ~70.2% mAP / 72.9% NDS on nuScenes test for detection and strong BEV map segmentation, at lower compute than prior fusion.
items.whyForManwe: Reference lidar-camera fusion architecture if crebain adds lidar. The shared-BEV paradigm is also the template for fusing crebain's heterogeneous sensors into a common frame before tracking. Heavily CUDA-optimized (custom BEV pooling / sparse conv) — a significant Metal/MPS porting effort.

items.name: TransFusion
items.whatItIs: Transformer-based lidar-camera fusion with a soft-association mechanism robust to degraded image conditions; convolutional backbones + transformer-decoder detection head. Achieved 1st place on the nuScenes tracking leaderboard at release.
items.whyForManwe: Query/attention-based fusion alternative to BEVFusion, notable for graceful degradation when one modality is poor — valuable for crebain where camera fails at night/fog and radar/acoustic must carry. Its soft association is conceptually aligned with probabilistic gating.

items.name: GLMB / LMB (labeled random finite set filters)
items.whatItIs: Generalized Labeled Multi-Bernoulli and Labeled Multi-Bernoulli filters solve the labeled multi-target Bayes recursion in exact closed form, natively modeling clutter, birth, spawn, death, and providing track labels/identity — superseding moment-approximation PHD/CPHD filters. 2025-2026 work connects GLMB to multitarget-correlation models.
items.whyForManwe: A labeled-RFS implementation could provide a principled research path for dense, clutter-heavy scenarios, but Stone Soup v1.9.1 does not implement GLMB. Any GLMB/PMBM experiment needs a separate implementation and independent reference fixtures; no real-time or Apple deployment claim follows.

items.name: PMBM / TPMBM and MM-TPMBM (Poisson Multi-Bernoulli Mixture)
items.whatItIs: PMBM and its trajectory version (TPMBM) give provably Bayes-optimal closed-form multi-target recursions separating detected (MB) from undetected (Poisson) targets. The 2024-2026 Multiple-Model Trajectory PMBM (MM-TPMBM) integrates jump-Markov dynamics for joint trajectory estimation and motion-model switching of maneuvering targets.
items.whyForManwe: State-of-the-art alternative to GLMB with often better computational scaling; MM-TPMBM's jump-Markov handling is exactly what maneuvering drones need (hover to dash). Reference MATLAB code exists in Garcia-Fernandez's MTT for cross-validation before any Rust/Python port.

items.name: GM-PHD / CPHD filters
items.whatItIs: Gaussian-Mixture PHD (and cardinalized CPHD) propagate the first-order moment of the multi-target posterior; analogous to a Kalman filter for sets. Handle unknown/varying target number, clutter, and birth/death without explicit data association. Implemented as a runnable tutorial in Stone Soup.
items.whyForManwe: Lightest-weight RFS entry point and a good clutter-robust baseline to benchmark against the CV+GNN tracker. Because it avoids hard association, it is a useful sanity check in high-false-alarm radar/acoustic regimes. Runs today inside the Stone Soup harness for apples-to-apples comparison.

items.name: IMM (Interacting Multiple Model) + JPDA
items.whatItIs: IMM runs a bank of motion models (e.g. constant-velocity + constant-turn + constant-acceleration) with Markov mode-switching for maneuvering targets; JPDA computes globally-consistent soft measurement-to-track association weights, effective under clutter and missed detections. Recent work fuses IMM with multi-Bernoulli filters (robust IMM-MB under glint/heavy-tailed noise).
items.whyForManwe: IMM and JPDA are research candidates for maneuvering and overlapping gates, not drop-in upgrades. Stone Soup v1.9.1 can provide JPDA and overlapping single-model filter references but not a classical IMM; an IMM needs independent equations, fixtures, and cross-validation before any port.

items.name: MOTR / MOTRv2 (transformer end-to-end tracking)
items.whatItIs: DETR-based end-to-end trackers using persistent track queries to model instances across a whole video, jointly learning detection + association. MOTRv2 bootstraps with a pretrained detector and Deformable DETR, reaching ~73.4% HOTA on DanceTrack (1st place, group-dance challenge); MOTIP ~71.4% HOTA. Later CO-MOT/MeMOTR/MOTRv3 refine the paradigm.
items.whyForManwe: Best-in-class on non-linear, similar-appearance motion (DanceTrack) — analogous to erratic swarming drones where motion-model trackers struggle. A candidate for the learned per-camera tracker, though heavier and CUDA-oriented; on Apple Silicon expect MPS via PyTorch with reduced throughput.

items.name: ByteTrack / OC-SORT / BoT-SORT (tracking-by-detection)
items.whatItIs: Lightweight Kalman-filter + association trackers. ByteTrack associates high- and low-score boxes to recover occluded objects; OC-SORT adds observation-centric re-update and momentum to fix Kalman error accumulation during occlusion; BoT-SORT adds camera-motion compensation and optional ReID. Remain competitive with and faster than transformer trackers on MOT17/MOT20.
items.whyForManwe: Pragmatic, real-time per-camera tracker for crebain edge nodes: cheap, well-understood Kalman core, same CV-filter + Mahalanobis/IoU association pattern crebain already uses. Available as pure-Rust crates for the Metal/CPU edge without a Python runtime.

items.name: Rust-native trackers: similari-trackers-rs, mot-rs, jamtrack-rs
items.whatItIs: Pure-Rust MOT crates. similari-trackers-rs: SORT with IoU or Mahalanobis distance and oriented/axis-aligned boxes. mot-rs: Kalman + ByteTrack (greedy or Hungarian). jamtrack-rs: ByteTrack, BoT-SORT, OC-SORT, BoostTrack with xywh Kalman, optional ECC camera compensation and ReID.
items.whyForManwe: Directly usable in manwe's Rust/Candle/Metal stack for the shipped real-time tracker — no Python at inference. similari's Mahalanobis SORT is the closest off-the-shelf match to crebain's gated CV tracker. None implement JPDA/IMM/RFS, so the fusion core stays hand-rolled Rust validated against the Stone Soup reference.

items.name: Multimodal Transformer for UAV Detection (radar + audio + video)
items.whatItIs: Transformer that fuses radar returns, acoustic signatures, and video to detect/recognize UAVs and aerial objects, learning cross-modal and temporal relationships jointly rather than per-modality. Paired trend: 2025 work also fuses cellular ISAC with passive RF sensing for UAV detection and tracking.
items.whyForManwe: Closest published analog to crebain's exact sensor mix (radar + acoustic + camera). Useful as a fusion-architecture reference and to justify a learned early-fusion front-end feeding the Bayesian tracker. Maturity is early/academic — validate claims before adopting.

items.name: Stone Soup multi-sensor + async extensions (context-aware sensor modeling; ADS-B MTT)
items.whatItIs: Recent papers demonstrating Stone Soup used for real asynchronous multi-sensor fusion: context-aware sensor modeling for asynchronous multi-sensor tracking, and ADS-B-based multi-target tracking with a Stochastic Integration Filter over OpenSky data. A FUSION 2025 special session covered Stone Soup applications.
items.whyForManwe: Evidence that Stone Soup scales beyond toy sims to heterogeneous, asynchronous, real-sensor fusion — de-risking it as crebain's simulation + evaluation harness. Provides worked patterns for async sensor timing crebain needs (camera 30 Hz vs radar 1 Hz vs acoustic bursts).

items.name: MOT17 / MOT20 / DanceTrack benchmarks (via TrackEval)
items.whatItIs: Dominant public MOT benchmarks: MOT17/MOT20 (crowded pedestrians), DanceTrack (non-linear, similar-appearance motion). All scored with HOTA/MOTA/IDF1 through TrackEval. DanceTrack is where transformer trackers currently lead (MOTRv2 ~73.4 HOTA).
items.whyForManwe: Standard yardsticks to benchmark crebain's visual tracker against published numbers and to sanity-check the harness. DanceTrack's fast non-linear motion is the closest public proxy for erratic drone maneuvering; nuScenes is the parallel yardstick for radar/lidar/camera fusion detectors.

keyClaims.claim: Stone Soup v1.9.1 (released 2026-06-24) is MIT-licensed pure Python and implements KF/EKF/UKF/particle filters, an IMM predictor, GNN/PDA/JPDA and MHT-style associators, GM-PHD and GLMB random-finite-set filters, Mahalanobis distance hypothesisers/gaters, M-of-N MultiMeasurementInitiator, and OSPA/GOSPA/SIAP metrics plus simulators — a complete model-based fusion simulation + evaluation harness, but with no GPU/CUDA/Metal acceleration (it explicitly prioritizes flexibility over performance).
keyClaims.correctedClaim: Stone Soup v1.9.1 is an MIT-licensed CPU Python framework with KF/EKF/UKF/PF, GNN/PDA/JPDA, GM-PHD, gating, M-of-N initiation, metrics, and simulators. It does not implement a classical IMM or GLMB filter, and MFA/PMHT should not be described as a general classical MHT implementation.

keyClaims.claim: HOTA is now the primary MOT metric (via TrackEval, MIT), but MOTA/MOTP/HOTA are not true mathematical metrics (they fail the identity and triangle-inequality properties), whereas GOSPA and T-GOSPA are mathematically consistent and decompose error into localization, missed, false, and (T-GOSPA) track-switch terms — making GOSPA the more principled primary metric for a Bayesian fusion tracker.

On nuScenes, lidar-camera BEVFusion (MIT, ICRA'23) reports ~70.2% mAP / 72.9% NDS, while camera-radar CRN (ICCV'23) reports 62.4% NDS / 57.5% mAP, ranks first among camera and camera-radar 3D detectors, and runs ~20 FPS real-time — establishing radar-camera BEV fusion (not lidar) as the most relevant learned-detector regime for a radar+camera counter-UAS stack.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: The cited CRN and BEVFusion figures are supported, but CRN's first-place framing was publication-time and is stale by mid-2026. Relevance to counter-UAS is an engineering hypothesis that needs target-domain validation.

keyClaims.claim: Random-finite-set SOTA has shifted from PHD/CPHD moment approximations to closed-form labeled/PMBM families (GLMB/LMB, PMBM/TPMBM), with 2024-2026 work adding jump-Markov multiple-model variants (MM-TPMBM) for joint trajectory estimation and motion-model switching of maneuvering targets; reference implementations exist (Garcia-Fernandez MTT has PMBM/MBM/PMB/PHD/CPHD + trajectory variants and GOSPA/T-GOSPA, but not GLMB/LMB).

On DanceTrack (fast non-linear motion), end-to-end transformer trackers now lead — MOTRv2 ~73.4% HOTA, MOTIP ~71.4% HOTA — but on MOT17/MOT20 pedestrian benchmarks tracking-by-detection methods (ByteTrack, OC-SORT) remain competitive and faster, and are available as pure-Rust crates (jamtrack-rs, mot-rs, similari-trackers-rs) for a Python-free real-time edge tracker.

No mature Rust library implements JPDA, IMM, or random-finite-set (GLMB/PMBM) filters — existing Rust crates are SORT/ByteTrack/OC-SORT-class Kalman trackers only (similari-trackers-rs supports Mahalanobis-distance SORT) — so crebain's model-based fusion core must be a Python/MATLAB reference (Stone Soup, Garcia-Fernandez MTT) cross-validating a hand-rolled Rust CV/IMM tracker.

recommendations: Evaluate Stone Soup as an independent offline fusion cross-check where algorithms and conventions overlap. Build a comparison tracker from ConstantVelocity 6-state (x,vx,y,vy,z,vz) + DistanceHypothesiser(Mahalanobis) chi-square gating + GNN (upgradeable to JPDA) associator + MultiMeasurementInitiator for M-of-N confirmation + a covariance/time-step deleter. Do not assume it mirrors another tracker 1:1; compare declared intermediate-state fixtures on identical seeded scenarios.

Use GOSPA (localization/missed/false decomposition) and T-GOSPA (adds track switches) as the primary accuracy metrics for the fusion filter, ported from Garcia-Fernandez's Python T-GOSPA / MTT spec; add TrackEval HOTA/MOTA/IDF1 for the video/multi-camera side scored against MOT-style ground truth. Wire both into CI so every tracker/detector change is regression-scored.

Keep the production, real-time tracker in Rust for the Metal/edge path. Start from similari-trackers-rs (Mahalanobis SORT, oriented boxes) or mot-rs, but validate its Kalman/gating math numerically against the Stone Soup reference on shared simulated scenarios before trusting it in the field. Do not expect JPDA/IMM/RFS from any crate — hand-roll those in Rust and validate against Stone Soup / Garcia-Fernandez MTT.

Evaluate IMM (CV + constant-turn + constant-acceleration with Markov mode switching) and JPDA as separate ablations for maneuvering and overlapping gates. Stone Soup can reference overlapping filter and JPDA components but not a classical IMM. Treat GM-PHD and separately sourced GLMB/PMBM implementations as distinct research tracks, cross-checked with independently specified fixtures and Garcia-Fernandez MTT where algorithms overlap.

For the learned fusion front-end feeding the tracker, evaluate CRN- or CenterFusion-style radar-camera BEV fusion (start with the simpler CenterFusion frustum association, then assess CRN's radar-guided BEV lifting). Reserve BEVFusion/TransFusion as lidar-camera references if lidar is added. Manwe currently offers raw ONNX/CoreML/TensorRT conversion only; MLX and every consumer adapter remain future work. These nets use custom CUDA BEV-pooling/sparse ops and need real re-implementation and measurement for MPS/MLX.

Build a synthetic scenario generator on Stone Soup's MultiTargetGroundTruthSimulator with configurable clutter density, detection probability, and birth/death, emitting correlated drone/bird/aircraft/helicopter tracks across multiple camera FOVs, acoustic azimuth/elevation bearings, and radar range-doppler returns (with realistic asynchronous sensor rates). This lets fusion, Mahalanobis gating, and the M-of-N lifecycle be stress-tested and GOSPA/T-GOSPA-scored before any field data exists.

Set maturity expectations explicitly in the repo: Stone Soup and Garcia-Fernandez MTT are research-grade offline references (no GPU, not real-time); the Rust crates are lightweight SORT/ByteTrack-class only; the multimodal radar+audio+video UAV transformers are early/academic. The realistic architecture is Python/MATLAB reference + eval on one side, and a hand-built, reference-validated Rust CV/IMM tracker (plus optional learned radar-camera detector) shipping on Metal/CUDA on the other.

verifications.claim: Stone Soup v1.9.1 (released 2026-06-24) is MIT-licensed pure Python and implements KF/EKF/UKF/particle filters, an IMM predictor, GNN/PDA/JPDA and MHT-style associators, GM-PHD and GLMB random-finite-set filters, Mahalanobis distance hypothesisers/gaters, M-of-N MultiMeasurementInitiator, and OSPA/GOSPA/SIAP metrics plus simulators — a complete model-based fusion simulation + evaluation harness, but with no GPU/CUDA/Metal acceleration (it explicitly prioritizes flexibility over performance).
verifications.rationale:
I verified this independently against the GitHub API, PyPI, the LICENSE, the design docs, and a fresh git checkout of the v1.9.1 source tree (grepped directly). The overall thesis and most enumerated features are CONFIRMED, but two specifically named capabilities are FABRICATED, and one characterization is overstated.

CONFIRMED: (1) Version/date — GitHub API gives v1.9.1 published_at 2026-06-24T13:57:38Z, exactly as claimed. (2) MIT license — the repo LICENSE file is verbatim "MIT License"; PyPI metadata agrees. (3) Pure Python — PyPI lists Python >=3.10; the package is NumPy/SciPy-backed with no compiled extensions of its own. (4) KF/EKF/UKF — stonesoup/updater/kalman.py defines KalmanUpdater, ExtendedKalmanUpdater, UnscentedKalmanUpdater (plus Cubature, Sqrt, Iterated, Schmidt, StochasticIntegration). (5) Particle filters — updater/particle.py, predictor/particle.py. (6) GNN/PDA/JPDA — dataassociator/neighbour.py (GlobalNearestNeighbour, GNNWith2DAssignment) and probability.py (PDA, JPDA). (7) GM-PHD — updater/pointprocess.py (PHDUpdater, LCCUpdater) with GaussianMixtureHypothesiser. (8) Mahalanobis gaters/hypothesisers — gater/distance.py (DistanceGater), hypothesiser/distance.py (DistanceHypothesiser), Mahalanobis measure in measures/state.py. (9) M-of-N MultiMeasurementInitiator — initiator/simple.py class MultiMeasurementInitiator with min_points property. (10) OSPA/GOSPA/SIAP — metricgenerator/ospametric.py (OSPAMetric, GOSPAMetric) and tracktotruthmetrics.py (SIAPMetrics, IDSIAPMetrics). (11) Simulators — simulator/simple.py (ground-truth and detection simulators). (12) No GPU/CUDA/Metal — the framework is CPU NumPy/SciPy with no GPU acceleration (only an optional `tensorflow` extra for a few neural components; core trackers are CPU-only).

FALSE / inaccurate: (A) "an IMM predictor" — there is NO Interacting Multiple Model filter. The strings "IMM"/"Interacting Multiple Model" do not appear as any predictor/updater class anywhere in the v1.9.1 tree (substring hits are coincidental, e.g. "Immutable", "trimmed"). Stone Soup instead has a multiple-model *particle* filter (MultiModelPredictor, RaoBlackwellisedMultiModelPredictor) — a distinct jump-Markov algorithm, not IMM; IMM/GPB is a known open gap. (B) "GLMB random-finite-set filters" — there is NO Generalised Labeled Multi-Bernoulli filter. The only "multi-Bernoulli" references are docstring citations in the MFA (multi-frame assignment) associator to the Trajectory PMBM paper. Stone Soup's actual RFS filters are GM-PHD, LCC-PHD, and a single-target Bernoulli particle filter (BernoulliParticleUpdater) — no GLMB (and no PMBM/LMB filter). So "GM-PHD and GLMB" overstates: GM-PHD yes, GLMB no. (C) "MHT-style associators" — loose: there is no classical Reid track-oriented MHT associator; the nearest analogues are the MFADataAssociator (N-scan multi-frame assignment) and a PMHTTracker. Defensible as "MHT-family" but not literal MHT. (D) "it explicitly prioritizes flexibility over performance" — the design page (readthedocs v1.9/design.html) states goals of interchangeability/modularity for academics and practitioners; it contains NO explicit statement that flexibility is prioritized over performance or that the framework is not optimised for speed. The "no GPU" fact is true, but the "explicitly prioritizes flexibility over performance" framing is an unsupported paraphrase, not a documented claim.

Net: the core claim (v1.9.1, 2026-06-24, MIT, pure Python, a broad model-based fusion sim+eval harness with KF/EKF/UKF/PF, GNN/PDA/JPDA, GM-PHD, Mahalanobis gating, M-of-N init, OSPA/GOSPA/SIAP, simulators, no GPU accel) is accurate and well-supported, but the specific inclusion of an IMM predictor and GLMB filters is incorrect, and the "explicitly prioritizes flexibility over performance" attribution is not documented. Hence partially-supported rather than supported or refuted. Note also that the claim's own cited tutorial URLs (GMPHDTutorial v1.6, JPDATutorial v0.1b9) only cover GM-PHD and JPDA and do not evidence the IMM or GLMB assertions.

verifications.claim: HOTA is now the primary MOT metric (via TrackEval, MIT), but MOTA/MOTP/HOTA are not true mathematical metrics (they fail the identity and triangle-inequality properties), whereas GOSPA and T-GOSPA are mathematically consistent and decompose error into localization, missed, false, and (T-GOSPA) track-switch terms — making GOSPA the more principled primary metric for a Bayesian fusion tracker.
verifications.rationale:
Every load-bearing sub-claim checks out against independent sources I retrieved directly.

(1) HOTA as primary MOT metric via TrackEval: CONFIRMED. TrackEval (github.com/JonathonLuiten/TrackEval) calls HOTA the "Recommended tracking metric," is the official reference implementation, and is used by KITTI/MOTChallenge. HOTA is widely described as the most popular MOT metric. The "MIT" tag is AMBIGUOUS but defensible: TrackEval is released under the MIT License (verified). If "MIT" was meant as the institution, it is WRONG — HOTA/TrackEval come from RWTH Aachen (Luiten, Leibe), TU Munich (Dendorfer, Leal-Taixé), Oxford (Torr), and Tübingen/MPI (Geiger); MIT the university is not an author affiliation. Read as the license, it is correct.

(2) MOTA/MOTP/HOTA are not true metrics (fail identity + triangle inequality): CONFIRMED essentially verbatim by arXiv:2507.13706 — "none of MOTA, MOTP or HOTA are mathematical metrics, as they do not meet the identity and triangle inequality properties" (it adds MOTA is also non-symmetric). Corroborated by the EmergentMind trajectory-metrics survey ("heuristic metrics do not satisfy the triangle inequality or even basic symmetry, so they do not constitute true mathematical metrics"). The HOTA paper itself does not claim metric-axiom status, consistent with this.

(3) GOSPA/T-GOSPA are mathematically consistent with the stated decomposition: CONFIRMED. The original GOSPA (arXiv:1601.05585) is a proven true metric — non-negativity, identity, and symmetry are immediate and the triangle inequality is proved in its Appendix A — and it decomposes into localization + missed + false terms. Trajectory GOSPA (T-GOSPA) adds a track-switch cost, exactly as claimed. One nuance worth flagging: the specifically cited arXiv:2507.13706 is titled "GOSPA and T-GOSPA quasi-metrics" and introduces variants that deliberately RELAX symmetry (to penalize missed vs. false differently), so those particular variants are quasi-metrics, not full metrics. The "mathematically consistent / true metric" property the claim relies on holds for the standard GOSPA/T-GOSPA (from arXiv:1601.05585 and the trajectory-GOSPA line), which the claim implicitly references — so the substance is correct, though the chosen citation is about the quasi-metric relaxation rather than the base metric.

(4) "GOSPA the more principled primary metric for a Bayesian fusion tracker": this is an evaluative recommendation, not a checkable fact, but it is well-founded. GOSPA is a genuine metric and is the de-facto standard in the Bayesian random-finite-set multi-target tracking community (PHD/CPHD/PMBM filters), which is precisely the domain of a KF/EKF/UKF/PF/IMM fusion tracker; HOTA dominates the computer-vision MOT-benchmark community instead. Calling GOSPA/T-GOSPA "more principled" for that use case is defensible; "primary" is a reasonable judgment rather than an established universal fact.

Net: the claim is factually accurate on all technical points, with only minor imprecisions (the ambiguous "MIT" parenthetical, and citing the quasi-metric paper for a property that belongs to the base GOSPA metric). None of these undermine the claim, so verdict = supported.

verifications.claim: On nuScenes, lidar-camera BEVFusion (MIT, ICRA'23) reports ~70.2% mAP / 72.9% NDS, while camera-radar CRN (ICCV'23) reports 62.4% NDS / 57.5% mAP, ranks first among camera and camera-radar 3D detectors, and runs ~20 FPS real-time — establishing radar-camera BEV fusion (not lidar) as the most relevant learned-detector regime for a radar+camera counter-UAS stack.
verifications.rationale:
Every numeric/factual sub-claim is accurate and correctly attributed to its cited source. BEVFusion (MIT, ICRA'23): the mit-han-lab/bevfusion GitHub model zoo reports the LiDAR+Camera fusion detector at 70.23 mAP / 72.88 NDS on the nuScenes test set (68.52/71.38 on val), matching the claimed "~70.2% mAP / 72.9% NDS"; the repo confirms ICRA'23. CRN (arXiv:2304.00670, ICCV'23): the abstract states verbatim "CRN with offline setting yields 62.4% NDS, 57.5% mAP on nuScenes test set and ranks first among all camera and camera-radar 3D object detectors" and "CRN with real-time setting operates at 20 FPS" — all three CRN sub-claims match exactly.

Two issues keep this from full support. (1) The "ranks first among camera and camera-radar 3D detectors" is a point-in-time claim true at ICCV'23 publication, but the claim states it in present tense as if still current in mid-2026. It has since been surpassed: RCBEVDet (CVPR'24) reports +2.8 NDS / +2.1 mAP over CRN on val; HyDRa (2024) reaches 61.7 NDS with ResNet101; CRT-Fusion (2024) and RaCFormer (CVPR'25) further improve; and RCBEVDet++ reports 72.73 NDS / 67.34 mAP. So CRN is no longer state of the art among camera-radar detectors. (2) The concluding inference — that these results "establish radar-camera BEV fusion (not lidar) as the most relevant learned-detector regime for a radar+camera counter-UAS stack" — is an unsupported extrapolation. Both cited sources are nuScenes autonomous-driving benchmarks (ground vehicles, automotive radar, road scenes); neither addresses counter-UAS, aerial small-target detection, or the different radar bands/geometries involved. The architectural analogy may be reasonable as engineering judgment, but it is the author's inference, not something "established" by these sources, and it glosses over that BEVFusion's higher lidar-camera scores actually show lidar fusion is more accurate on nuScenes — the radar-camera choice is a cost/coverage tradeoff, not an accuracy-driven "most relevant" conclusion. Verdict: the benchmark numbers are supported and honestly sourced; the currency of the "ranks first" framing and the counter-UAS conclusion are not, hence partially-supported.

verifications.claim: Random-finite-set SOTA has shifted from PHD/CPHD moment approximations to closed-form labeled/PMBM families (GLMB/LMB, PMBM/TPMBM), with 2024-2026 work adding jump-Markov multiple-model variants (MM-TPMBM) for joint trajectory estimation and motion-model switching of maneuvering targets; reference implementations exist (Garcia-Fernandez MTT has PMBM/MBM/PMB/PHD/CPHD + trajectory variants and GOSPA/T-GOSPA, but not GLMB/LMB).
verifications.rationale: All three components verified independently, and all three cited URLs are real and support the claim parts they are attached to. (1) The PHD/CPHD-as-moment-approximations vs. labeled/PMBM-as-full-density-conjugate-families taxonomy is a standard, well-established characterization of the field; corroborated by García-Fernández's trajectory PMB work (arxiv 2003.12767) and the labeled-vs-trajectory RFS comparison (arxiv 2401.17314). (2) MM-TPMBM is confirmed by PMC12714792 = Salim, Okasha, Barbary, Anis, 'Multiple model trajectory Poisson multi-Bernoulli mixtures filter for tracking multiple maneuvering objects,' Scientific Reports, 17 Dec 2025: it embeds jump Markov system (JMS) dynamics in the trajectory RFS framework, derives closed-form prediction/update, does joint trajectory estimation + motion-model switching, and benchmarks against TPMBM, MM-PMBM and delta-GLMB (best mean total GOSPA 2.263). An extended-target sibling (MM-ET-TPMBM) also exists (IET Radar, Sonar & Navigation 2026). (3) Fetching github.com/Agarciafernandez/MTT confirms it implements PMBM, MBM, PMB, PHD, CPHD plus trajectory variants (TPMBM/TPMB/TMBM/TMB/TPHD/TCPHD) and both GOSPA and T-GOSPA metrics, with no GLMB/LMB filters (those are Vo & Vo's separate codebases). Attribution nuance worth noting: the cited arxiv 2506.02772 is NOT the MM-TPMBM paper but Ronald Mahler's June-2025 'Generalized Labeled Multi-Bernoulli Filters and Multitarget-Correlation Models' — it legitimately supports the GLMB/LMB (labeled-family) portion of the claim while PMC12714792 supports the MM-TPMBM portion, so each source backs the sub-claim it belongs to. Minor honesty caveats that do not refute the claim: 'shifted' slightly overstates the picture since PHD/CPHD remain active baselines (extended-target, low-compute), and LMB is formally an approximation to GLMB rather than a fully closed-form conjugate prior.

topic: SOTA training/fine-tuning recipes and model export toolchains (2024-2026) for crebain's four backends (ONNX, CoreML .mlmodelc, MLX safetensors, TensorRT)
summary: This section surveys a possible training and export toolchain; it is not Manwe's implementation inventory. Manwe currently constructs supported architectures from random initialization, disables backend-managed checkpoint downloads, and has no local-checkpoint fine-tuning adapter. It provides raw ONNX, CoreML, and TensorRT conversion behind explicit trust acknowledgements, but no MLX converter and no drop-in crebain adapter. SAHI sliced inference is implemented; slicing-aided training, a P2 head, distillation, and four-backend fan-out remain proposals. Every produced artifact still requires backend inspection, an exact digest, preprocessing and tensor fixtures, accuracy/fidelity gates, and a consumer-owned adapter.

items.name: Ultralytics YOLO26
items.whatItIs: Ultralytics' current stable flagship detector family (n/s/m/l/x, 6 tasks). NMS-free dual-head (one-to-one head is default, max 300 dets/img), DFL removed for a lighter head, trained with the MuSGD optimizer, Progressive Loss, and STAL label assignment. 40.9-57.5 mAP on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT; up to ~43% faster CPU ONNX than YOLO11n; engineered for INT8 quantization robustness.
items.whyForManwe: A tooling-track research candidate for the local five-class taxonomy. A successful upstream export does not imply identical FP16/FP32 results or consumer compatibility. Manwe supports raw ONNX/CoreML/TensorRT conversion only; MLX and consumer adapters remain unimplemented.

items.name: Ultralytics export API (model.export)
items.whatItIs: Single-call exporter to 18 formats including ONNX, TensorRT (.engine), CoreML (.mlpackage), TorchScript, OpenVINO. Args: quantize (8/16/32, w8a16), dynamic, nms, simplify (onnxslim), opset, batch, workspace, data (calibration set), fraction, end2end. Per-format precision matrix documents which backends support FP16/INT8/W8A16.
items.whyForManwe: Manwe exposes separate raw ONNX, CoreML, and TensorRT conversion operations through the upstream API. That is not one portable consumer path: each output needs backend inspection, a digest-bound contract, semantic fixtures, and a crebain adapter.

items.name: RF-DETR (Roboflow Detection Transformer)
items.whatItIs: Real-time DETR with a DINOv2 ViT backbone, ICLR 2026, Apache-2.0 for Nano-through-Large and PML-1.0 for XL/2XL. RF-DETR-L reports 56.5 AP at 6.8 ms T4 and 2XL reports 60.1 AP on COCO. Cross-family comparisons require one matched evaluation protocol.
items.whyForManwe: The accuracy ceiling for crebain's vision detector and a strong transfer-learning base for small/rare drone classes. NMS-free like YOLO26, ONNX/TensorRT-exportable, and its DINOv2 features generalize well on out-of-distribution aerial scenes. Keep as a parallel high-accuracy track alongside YOLO26.

items.name: yolo-mlx (YOLO26-MLX by webAI)
items.whatItIs: Native Apple MLX implementation of YOLO26 with training, inference, and weight-conversion tools and no PyTorch runtime. Its parity and M4 Pro speed figures are vendor self-reported, changed across project revisions, and have not been independently reproduced; benchmark the exact revision and official baseline.
items.whyForManwe: This is crebain's MLX/safetensors backend path and the only mature way to TRAIN detection natively on Apple Silicon (vs. MPS). Removes the PyTorch dependency at runtime on Macs. Maturity caveat: newest link in the chain, conversion tooling may still need PyTorch.

items.name: NVIDIA TensorRT + ModelOpt explicit quantization
items.whatItIs: TensorRT builds .engine files with FP32/FP16/INT8. TensorRT 11 removed implicit quantization and the IInt8Calibrator interface; Ultralytics now uses NVIDIA ModelOpt explicit quantization that inserts Q/DQ nodes into the ONNX graph and builds a strongly-typed engine. Calibration needs ~500-1000 representative images via data=; dynamic shapes auto-enabled with quantize=8. TensorRT 11 dropped DLA (use 10.x for DLA).
items.whyForManwe: The NVIDIA-side crebain backend. INT8 is where the throughput win lives but also where accuracy silently drops, so calibration must use manwe's own drone imagery, not COCO. NVIDIA-only: there is no Apple equivalent, so this branch is CUDA-exclusive.

items.name: coremltools 8 (CoreML .mlpackage/.mlmodelc + optimize)
items.whatItIs: Converts to CoreML and compresses via palettization (1-8 bit LUT, best on ANE), INT4 per-block, and iOS18 per-grouped-channel palettization (group_size shares a LUT). Joint compression: quantize weights to int8 then palettize to a 4-bit LUT with int8 entries (A8W4) to hit the fast int8-int8 ANE compute path on A17 Pro/M4. coremltools.optimize.torch supports PTQ and QAT.
items.whyForManwe: A candidate route from a raw CoreML package to a compiled deployment artifact. The removed historical harness did not establish a reproducible CoreML-versus-Metal ranking, so no throughput ratio is claimed. Pin feature names, compute units, precision, placement, and semantic fixtures on the target OS and hardware.

items.name: ONNX + ONNX Runtime (opset/simplify/QDQ PTQ)
items.whatItIs: Interchange format and the hub of the export graph. Best practice: pin an opset, run onnxslim/simplify, choose static vs dynamic axes deliberately, and quantize with QDQ (static PTQ with a calibration reader). ONNX Runtime is also crebain's CPU/cross-platform inference and the entry point to TensorRT and CoreML EPs.
items.whyForManwe: ONNX is the pivot: every other crebain backend (TensorRT, and CoreML via conversion) starts here, so getting opset/dynamic-shape/NMS choices right once prevents downstream fidelity loss. Keep NMS external for max FP fidelity; validate the ONNX against the PyTorch reference before fanning out.

items.name: Microsoft Olive
items.whatItIs: AI model optimization toolkit for ONNX Runtime (v0.8.0, Mar 2025). Runs configurable optimization 'passes' (conversion, graph opt, quantization) and a search over algorithms (static/dynamic OnnxQuantization, AWQ, GPTQ, QuaRot) targeting CPU/GPU/NPU. New Olive CLI simplifies the workflow.
items.whyForManwe: Automates the ONNX quantization/optimization sweep so manwe can auto-tune INT8 recipes per target device rather than hand-tuning. Useful for the CPU/NPU deployment tier and for producing calibrated QDQ ONNX that feeds TensorRT and CoreML.

items.name: SAHI (Slicing Aided Hyper Inference)
items.whatItIs: Framework-agnostic sliced/tiled inference AND fine-tuning for small objects: partition high-res frames into overlapping patches, detect per-patch, merge. Works over Ultralytics/RT-DETR/HF/torchvision models. Reported +5-7% AP for small objects on VisDrone/xView; 2025 work adds altitude-aware dynamic tiling for UAV imagery.
items.whyForManwe: SAHI is relevant to tiny-object experiments. Manwe currently wraps bounded sliced inference only; slicing-aided training remains a proposed ablation and no recall gain is assumed without representative evaluation.

items.name: AlbumentationsX
items.whatItIs: Actively-maintained successor to Albumentations (legacy MIT repo archived Jun 2025; AlbumentationsX is AGPL-3.0/commercial dual-licensed, API-compatible, faster). Bbox-aware transforms that update pixels and coordinates together; supports HBB and OBB, multiple coord formats.
items.whyForManwe: Domain augmentation beyond Ultralytics' built-ins: fog/rain/motion-blur/ISO-noise/brightness for thermal-vs-visible and adverse-weather robustness that a counter-UAS sensor faces. Note the AGPL license implication for a commercial crebain build.

items.name: Mosaic + MixUp + Copy-Paste (with close-mosaic)
items.whatItIs: Core detector augmentation stack: Mosaic tiles 4 images to boost scale/context diversity, MixUp/CutMix blend samples, Copy-Paste pastes instances to balance rare classes. Standard practice disables Mosaic for the final ~10-20 epochs (close_mosaic) to recover clean-image alignment; smaller models use weaker Mosaic scale and often drop MixUp.
items.whyForManwe: Copy-Paste directly addresses crebain's class imbalance (few drone instances vs many birds/aircraft) and Mosaic multiplies small-object exposure. close_mosaic matters for the final localization precision needed for triangulation/track handoff.

items.name: Knowledge distillation for detection (FGD / logit KD)
items.whatItIs: Compress a large teacher into a fast student. Feature-based FGD separates foreground/background and uses attention-guided supervision; logit-based KD raises distillation temperature. 2024-2025 CNN->transformer survey and UAV-specific progressive KD narrow the teacher-student small-object gap; e.g. YOLOv5l->YOLOv5s KD improves student mAP.
items.whyForManwe: Lets manwe ship an edge-sized n/s detector at the roll-up accuracy of an x-scale or RF-DETR teacher - critical when the same model must hit real-time on Jetson (TensorRT) and ANE. UAV KD papers show it specifically helps tiny-drone recall.

items.name: Model EMA + cosine/one-cycle LR + AMP
items.whatItIs: Reproducible recipe primitives: exponential moving average of weights (e.g. decay ~0.9999) smooths the loss landscape for better generalization at no extra cost; cosine/SGDR or one-cycle LR schedules; AMP mixed precision. PyTorch Lightning exposes a generic WeightAveraging callback (SWA/EMA) and timm provides cosine/SGDR schedulers.
items.whyForManwe: These are the low-risk, high-yield knobs that stabilize a config-driven training run and are already YOLO26/Ultralytics defaults; standardizing them (and logging the EMA decay + schedule) is what makes manwe runs reproducible across Metal/MPS and CUDA.

items.name: Experiment tracking: Weights & Biases / MLflow / Aim
items.whatItIs: W&B: polished hosted tracking+viz+Reports, native Ultralytics callback, strong collaboration. MLflow: open-source end-to-end lifecycle (tracking + model registry + autolog), self-hostable. Aim: lightweight open-source local-first alternative. 2025 trend is teams migrating toward W&B for UX and MLflow for open self-hosted registry.
items.whyForManwe: Config-driven reproducibility for crebain needs every run to log the exact augmentation seed, LR schedule, EMA decay, calibration-set hash, and per-backend export-fidelity metrics. W&B via the Ultralytics integration is the least-friction start; MLflow if self-hosting/air-gap is required.

items.name: timm / PyTorch Lightning / HF Trainer / MMDetection
items.whatItIs: Alternative training stacks when going beyond Ultralytics: timm for backbones/schedulers/EMA and SOTA pretraining primitives; PyTorch Lightning for structured config-driven loops (WeightAveraging, custom scheduler steps); HF Transformers Trainer for DETR-family models; MMDetection for a broad detector zoo/config system.
items.whyForManwe: For RF-DETR / DETR fine-tuning and for custom multi-modal (thermal/acoustic-conditioned) heads, manwe will need Lightning/HF-Trainer-style loops rather than the closed Ultralytics API. timm supplies pretrained backbones and battle-tested cosine/EMA components.

items.name: Anti-UAV benchmarks (Anti-UAV410, DUT Anti-UAV, CST Anti-UAV)
items.whatItIs: Counter-UAS detection/tracking datasets. DUT Anti-UAV: 10k detection images + 20 tracking sequences. Anti-UAV410: 410 thermal-IR sequences, 438k drone objects. CST Anti-UAV (2025): thermal-IR tiny-UAV tracking in complex scenes, targets <0.01% frame area. Original Anti-UAV: 318 visible+IR dual-mode videos.
items.whyForManwe: Directly on-target training/eval data for crebain's drone class, including the thermal modality crebain fuses. Use for transfer-learning fine-tuning, SAHI sliced training, and as the export-fidelity/mAP validation set that gates each backend.

items.name: TensorRT/ONNX INT8 PTQ vs QAT for YOLO
items.whatItIs: PTQ calibrates activation ranges from representative data post-training (fast, ~500-1000 imgs); QAT inserts fake-quant nodes during training for higher INT8 accuracy at more cost. Community guidance: custom-trained YOLO needs its own calibration set, not COCO; NMS-free/DFL-free graphs (YOLO26) quantize with minimal mAP loss.
items.whyForManwe: crebain's INT8 accuracy on drones depends entirely on calibrating with manwe's own imagery; start with PTQ and escalate to QAT only if the export-fidelity harness shows unacceptable mAP drop on small/distant targets.

keyClaims.claim: YOLO26 (released Jan 2026) is NMS-free with a dual-head design, removes DFL, and trains with MuSGD + Progressive Loss + STAL; it achieves 40.9-57.5 mAP on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT and is engineered so INT8 exports retain near-FP32 accuracy.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: The release date, architecture, training recipe, and published FP16 AP/latency figures are supported. Quantization-friendly design does not prove near-FP32 INT8 accuracy; every exported artifact needs a measured fidelity gate.

keyClaims.claim: RF-DETR (ICLR 2026, Apache-2.0 for Nano-Large) is the first real-time detector to exceed 60 AP on COCO (RF-DETR-2XL 60.1 AP50:95); RF-DETR-L reaches 56.5 AP at 6.8 ms on a T4, beating YOLOv11x (54.7 AP) at lower latency.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: RF-DETR's reported figures are supported, but the 60.1-AP 2XL tier is PML-1.0 rather than Apache-2.0, and the 54.7 YOLOv11x comparison mixes evaluation protocols. Compare models only under one matched protocol.

keyClaims.claim: The native MLX port yolo-mlx trains and runs YOLO26 on Apple Silicon within ~0.2% (max 0.5%) mAP of the official model on COCO val2017, and on an M4 Pro delivers 1.1-2.6x faster inference and up to ~1.7x faster training than PyTorch MPS with no PyTorch runtime.
keyClaims.correctedClaim: The port exists, but its performance and parity figures are vendor self-reported and changed between its launch post and later README. Sub-0.5% parity is against its own re-validation; the gap to Ultralytics' published baseline can reach about 1.1 AP. Re-benchmark independently.

keyClaims.claim: TensorRT 11 removed implicit quantization and the IInt8Calibrator interface; Ultralytics now performs INT8 via NVIDIA ModelOpt explicit quantization (inserting Q/DQ nodes into the ONNX graph and building a strongly-typed engine), and NVIDIA recommends roughly 500-1000 representative calibration images from the target dataset.

coremltools 8 adds iOS18/macOS15 per-grouped-channel palettization and INT4 per-block compression, and supports joint int8-then-4-bit-LUT (A8W4) that runs on the faster int8-int8 Neural Engine compute path on A17 Pro / M4 SoCs.

CoreML export has fidelity constraints: setting minimum_deployment_target >= iOS16 forces compute_precision=FLOAT32 because CoreML NMS layers do not accept FP16 input, and end2end quantized YOLO exports keep output0 in FP32 to preserve class-index fidelity.

recommendations: Roadmap: evaluate a digest-bound local-checkpoint adapter before proposing one checkpoint as a source of truth. The current config-driven paths construct architectures from random initialization; no HF Trainer/Lightning fine-tuning track or pretrained local-checkpoint path is implemented.

Ablate each augmentation on representative data. Manwe exposes bounded Ultralytics augmentation fields and SAHI sliced inference; it does not implement AlbumentationsX integration or slicing-aided training. Review all optional dependency licenses before use.

Adopt the low-risk recipe primitives as defaults and log them: model EMA, cosine or one-cycle LR, AMP, and MuSGD (YOLO26 default) or SGD. Add optional FGD-style knowledge distillation from a YOLO26x or RF-DETR-L teacher into n/s students so the edge model (Jetson/ANE) keeps large-model recall on tiny drones.

Treat four-backend fan-out as a roadmap, not a current capability: Manwe can emit one raw ONNX, TensorRT, or CoreML candidate per invocation, has no MLX converter, and does not compile or validate a consumer adapter. Pin every conversion option and artifact digest, then require backend-specific tensor inspection, calibration evidence, fidelity gates, and consumer fixtures.

Add an export-fidelity harness that compares each exported backend against the PyTorch reference on a held-out drone/thermal validation set (Anti-UAV410 / DUT / CST) - per-detection deltas plus mAP - and gate the export on a tolerance (e.g. <0.5% mAP drop, tighter for INT8 on small objects). Treat CoreML FP16/palettization and TensorRT INT8 as the drift-prone paths.

Wire experiment tracking from day one: W&B via the native Ultralytics callback (or self-hosted MLflow if air-gapped), logging config, augmentation seeds, EMA decay, LR schedule, calibration-set hash, and the export-fidelity metrics so every crebain model artifact is reproducible.

Be explicit in the repo about platform gaps: TensorRT is NVIDIA/CUDA-only (no Apple path); MLX training is the newest and least battle-tested link and its weight-conversion tooling may still need PyTorch; and CoreML INT4/palettization ANE speedups depend on A17 Pro / M4-class SoCs. Optionally layer Microsoft Olive to auto-sweep ONNX INT8 recipes for the CPU/NPU tier.

verifications.claim: YOLO26 (released Jan 2026) is NMS-free with a dual-head design, removes DFL, and trains with MuSGD + Progressive Loss + STAL; it achieves 40.9-57.5 mAP on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT and is engineered so INT8 exports retain near-FP32 accuracy.
verifications.rationale: Independent verification confirms almost the entire claim, with one overstated element. CONFIRMED (via official Ultralytics docs, the genuine arxiv paper 2606.03748, and the official launch blog): (1) NMS-free + dual-head — YOLO26 uses a default one-to-one head that produces predictions without NMS (max 300 detections) plus an optional one-to-many head that uses NMS for slightly higher accuracy; (2) removes DFL entirely for a lighter, more quantization/export-friendly head; (3) trains with MuSGD (hybrid Muon-SGD), Progressive Loss (shifts supervision to the inference-time one-to-one head), and STAL (small-target-aware label assignment); (4) 40.9-57.5 mAP on COCO at 1.7-11.8 ms T4 TensorRT — matches the official docs and the paper abstract exactly (published T4 TensorRT10 FP16 figures across the n/s/m/l/x scales). RELEASE DATE is essentially correct but nuanced: YOLO26 was previewed/announced at YOLO Vision 2025 on Sept 25, 2025, then officially launched with downloadable weights in mid-January 2026 (official blog dated Jan 14, 2026; ultralytics/assets 'New YOLO26 Models Release' in mid-Jan 2026), so 'released Jan 2026' is defensible for the actual model release. The cited arxiv 2606.03748 is a REAL Ultralytics paper (Glenn Jocher et al., submitted 2 Jun 2026; cross-checked on arxiv, HuggingFace papers, ResearchGate) — a retrospective technical report rather than the release event, but it does support the architecture/training/benchmark claims. THE WEAK LINK is 'engineered so INT8 exports retain near-FP32 accuracy.' The design INTENT of quantization-friendliness is genuine and documented (DFL removal + NMS-free eliminate custom operators that are brittle to quantize), but the specific OUTCOME claim that INT8 retains near-FP32 accuracy is not substantiated by any of the three cited sources: the official YOLO26 docs and the arxiv paper make no such INT8 claim, and the cited TensorRT integration page actually presents INT8 as an accuracy trade-off ('metrics likely somewhat worse,' 'decreases in evaluation metrics'). The only near-FP32-INT8 assertion I found came from a third-party analysis paper (arxiv 2509.25164), not the cited primary sources. Because roughly six of seven components are accurate and well-sourced while the INT8 near-FP32 accuracy assertion is unsupported by (and mildly contradicted by) the cited sources, the overall claim is partially-supported rather than fully supported.

verifications.claim: RF-DETR (ICLR 2026, Apache-2.0 for Nano-Large) is the first real-time detector to exceed 60 AP on COCO (RF-DETR-2XL 60.1 AP50:95); RF-DETR-L reaches 56.5 AP at 6.8 ms on a T4, beating YOLOv11x (54.7 AP) at lower latency.
verifications.rationale:
Nearly every component checks out against primary sources, but one comparative number is inconsistent with the cited sources.

SUPPORTED: (1) ICLR 2026 acceptance is confirmed by the arxiv abstract (2511.09554), OpenReview (qHm5GePxTh), the ICLR 2026 poster page, and the GitHub repo title. (2) "First real-time detector to exceed 60 AP on COCO" is the paper's own peer-reviewed claim — the arxiv abstract states verbatim "RF-DETR (2x-large) is the first real-time detector to surpass 60 AP on COCO," and the official benchmark table lists RF-DETR-2XL at 60.1 AP50:95 (17.2 ms, T4 TensorRT FP16). (3) RF-DETR-L = 56.5 AP50:95 at 6.8 ms on T4 (TensorRT 10.4, CUDA 12.4, FP16, batch 1) is confirmed on the docs benchmark page. (4) License scoping is accurate: GitHub states standard sizes (Nano/Small/Medium/Large) are Apache 2.0 while XL/2XL are PML 1.0 — so "Apache-2.0 for Nano-Large" is correct and correctly excludes the 60.1-AP 2XL model.

DEFECT: The claim attributes 54.7 AP to YOLOv11x. RF-DETR's own benchmarks (both the docs benchmark page and the repo) instead report YOLOv11x at 50.9 AP50:95 (10.5 ms, T4 TensorRT FP16), stating verbatim "RF-DETR-L outperforms YOLOv11x (56.5 vs 50.9 AP50:95) at lower latency (6.8 vs 10.5 ms)." The 54.7 figure is the Ultralytics-official YOLO11x mAP50-95 (confirmed via Ultralytics docs), measured under a different protocol — not the number the RF-DETR comparison uses. So the claim mixes measurement sources (RF-DETR's own 56.5 vs Ultralytics' 54.7), an apples-to-oranges comparison inconsistent with the cited RF-DETR pages. The directional conclusion (RF-DETR-L beats YOLOv11x at lower latency) still holds under either number, but the specific "54.7 AP" is a factual inaccuracy relative to the cited sources.

CAVEAT ON SOURCES: One cited source, blog.roboflow.com/rf-detr, is the original March-2025 announcement (RF-DETR base 29M / large 128M, 60.5 mAP at 728 res) and does not contain the 2XL, RF-DETR-L 56.5, or YOLOv11x comparison figures at all; those specifics trace to the newer NAS paper (arxiv 2511.09554) and the docs benchmark page rather than the cited blog.

verifications.claim: The native MLX port yolo-mlx trains and runs YOLO26 on Apple Silicon within ~0.2% (max 0.5%) mAP of the official model on COCO val2017, and on an M4 Pro delivers 1.1-2.6x faster inference and up to ~1.7x faster training than PyTorch MPS with no PyTorch runtime.
verifications.rationale:
The project is real but the numbers are vendor self-reported, internally inconsistent, and the accuracy-parity claim is weaker than stated against the true official baseline.

CHECKS OUT: YOLO26 is a genuine Ultralytics model (released 2025-09-25; arxiv 2509.25164; docs.ultralytics.com/models/yolo26). yolo-mlx by webAI is a genuine open-source project (github.com/thewebAI/yolo-mlx, AGPL-3.0, ~193 stars, v0.4.0), a from-scratch MLX reimplementation with no PyTorch runtime. The claim faithfully paraphrases what webAI publishes: the launch blog does state 1.1-2.6x inference, up to ~1.7x training on M4 Pro, and mAP within ~0.2% (max 0.5%) on COCO val2017.

WHY ONLY PARTIALLY SUPPORTED (independent findings):
1) NO INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION. Every figure is webAI's own internal benchmarks. The only non-webAI source (mike.gold) is a repost of Awni Hannun's X bookmark of webAI's announcement, not a reproduction. The one arxiv MLX-vs-PyTorch Apple-Silicon paper (2510.18921) benchmarks BERT/RoBERTa, not YOLO. No third party has reproduced these numbers.
2) INTERNALLY INCONSISTENT. The launch blog (which the claim quotes) says 1.1-2.6x inference / ~1.7x training with MLX FPS n=124.9,s=57.7,m=25.3,l=19.8,x=10.7. The current repo README (v0.4.0) instead reports up to 2.07x inference (170.6 vs 82.6 FPS) and up to 2.65x training (64.1s vs 169.8s) with much higher MLX FPS. The claim's 1.1-2.6x/~1.7x are already contradicted by the project's own later README.
3) ACCURACY PARITY OVERSTATED VS ACTUAL OFFICIAL NUMBERS. webAI's baseline labeled Official mAP50-95 is n=40.1/s=47.8/m=52.5/l=54.4/x=56.9, but Ultralytics published n=40.9/s=48.6/m=53.1/l=55.0/x=57.5, i.e. webAI's official column is ~0.6-0.8 low. Against Ultralytics' real figures the MLX port (40.2/47.6/52.3/53.9/56.7) is ~0.7-1.1 mAP lower (up to ~1.1%), exceeding the claimed max 0.5%. The within-0.5% holds only vs webAI's own PyTorch re-validation of the same weights (a valid port-fidelity metric), not vs the official SOTA model as the phrasing implies.

NET: directionally true and traceable to sources, but presents unverified single-vendor numbers as fact, quotes figures the project itself has since revised, and the sub-0.5% parity holds only against a self-run baseline below Ultralytics' published numbers. Treat the speedups and parity as plausible-but-unconfirmed and version/config dependent; independent re-benchmarking on target Apple-Silicon hardware is warranted.

verifications.claim: TensorRT 11 removed implicit quantization and the IInt8Calibrator interface; Ultralytics now performs INT8 via NVIDIA ModelOpt explicit quantization (inserting Q/DQ nodes into the ONNX graph and building a strongly-typed engine), and NVIDIA recommends roughly 500-1000 representative calibration images from the target dataset.
verifications.rationale: All three technical assertions were independently verified against primary sources, not just the cited ones. (1) The NVIDIA TensorRT 11.0.0 Release Notes state verbatim that "Implicit quantization has been removed" and that "The IInt8Calibrator class and all subclasses (IInt8EntropyCalibrator, IInt8EntropyCalibrator2, IInt8MinMaxCalibrator, IInt8LegacyCalibrator), setInt8Calibrator(), setDynamicRange(), resetDynamicRange(), and all related calibration and dynamic-range APIs have been removed," directing users to explicit quantization with Q/DQ nodes via the TensorRT Model Optimizer — so the first assertion is exactly correct. (2) The current Ultralytics TensorRT integration docs state verbatim: "On TensorRT 11 and newer, Ultralytics performs INT8 quantization with NVIDIA ModelOpt explicit quantization, which inserts Q/DQ nodes into the ONNX graph before building a strongly-typed engine" — so the ModelOpt/Q/DQ/strongly-typed-engine assertion is exactly correct. (3) The Ultralytics docs attribute a calibration-image recommendation to NVIDIA: "Experimentation by NVIDIA led them to recommend using at least 500 calibration images that are representative of the data for your model," adding it is a guideline not a hard requirement. This makes the claim's "500" lower bound, the NVIDIA attribution, and the "representative of the target dataset" qualifier accurate; the "1000" upper bound is a mild paraphrase (the documented figure is an open-ended "at least 500"), though it is corroborated by other NVIDIA-forum and Ultralytics community sources that suggest 1000 or more. Caveat on citations: the first cited source (Ultralytics TensorRT docs) fully substantiates the claim, but the second cited source (GitHub issue ultralytics/ultralytics#11848) is only a user question about YOLOv8 INT8 calibration dataset size, closed as not planned/stale, and does NOT mention ModelOpt, IInt8Calibrator removal, Q/DQ nodes, or the 500-1000 figure. Net: the claim's substance is independently confirmed by NVIDIA and Ultralytics primary documentation; the only imprecision is treating "at least 500" as a "500-1000 range."

items.name: PyTorch MPS backend (Metal) + CUDA backend
items.whatItIs: The same PyTorch codebase runs on Apple Silicon via the MPS (Metal Performance Shaders) backend and on NVIDIA via CUDA/cuDNN. MPS is officially beta; CUDA is the reference backend.
items.whyForManwe: This is the single most portable train-and-export path for manwe: write detector/acoustic/fusion nets once, run on Metal for local Apple-Silicon dev and on CUDA for canonical training. But MPS training materially lags CUDA (no float64, no native FP8/FP4, only Gloo not NCCL for multi-GPU, silent CPU fallbacks), so treat CUDA as the production training path and MPS as dev/validation.

items.name: ONNX Runtime with CoreML / CUDA / TensorRT execution providers
items.whatItIs: Cross-platform inference engine that runs one ONNX graph through hardware-specific execution providers: CoreML EP (Apple ANE/GPU), CUDA EP (cuDNN), and TensorRT EP (whole-graph optimized engines).
items.whyForManwe: Directly satisfies crebain's ONNX + CoreML + TensorRT export targets from a single artifact. Export YOLO detectors once to ONNX, then serve with CoreML EP on Apple and TensorRT/CUDA EP on NVIDIA — the cleanest way to cover both hardware families without maintaining separate inference code.

items.name: NVIDIA TensorRT / TensorRT-EP
items.whatItIs: NVIDIA's graph-optimizing inference compiler. Reorders/fuses ops, picks kernels, and serializes an 'engine'; supports FP16/INT8. Engines are GPU-architecture-specific and must be pre-built and cached.
items.whyForManwe: The fastest NVIDIA deployment path for crebain's edge/GPU boxes. Ultralytics reports up to ~5x GPU speedup vs baseline and ~4x model-size reduction with INT8. Build engines at install time and cache per compute capability; fall back to CUDA EP where TensorRT lacks an op.

items.name: Core ML + coremltools 9.0
items.whatItIs: Apple's on-device inference stack (CPU/GPU/ANE) plus the converter. v9.0 (Nov 2025) adds PyTorch 2.7 and ExecuTorch 0.5 support, Python 3.13, int8 model I/O, and iOS26/macOS26 targets. Four-bit quantization, three-bit palettization, and stateful models arrived in v8.0. Core ML compiles .mlpackage bundles to .mlmodelc.
items.whyForManwe: A candidate path to .mlmodelc and ANE acceleration. Historical Manwe cross-backend figures used incomparable timing scopes and are not decision evidence; validate semantic parity, timed boundaries, and actual compute-unit placement on target hardware.

items.name: Apple MLX (mlx, mlx.nn, mlx.optimizers)
items.whatItIs: Apple's native array/training framework for Apple Silicon with a lazy graph, unified-memory model, PyTorch-like API, and safetensors-native weights.
items.whyForManwe: Directly hits crebain's MLX-safetensors export target and is the best Apple-side training/inference performance (2-3x faster generation than PyTorch MPS in several benchmarks). Good for on-device fine-tuning of small acoustic/fusion nets; verify numerical parity against the CUDA-trained reference.

items.name: Candle (Rust, Metal + CUDA)
items.whatItIs: HuggingFace's minimalist Rust ML framework with CPU/CUDA/Metal backends; inference-first with quantization support. Training exists but is basic; distributed/multi-GPU is still on the roadmap.
items.whyForManwe: manwe already ships Candle/Metal YOLOv8 inference; the same code compiles for CUDA, giving a Rust inference path on both hardware families. Keep Candle for inference; do not rely on it for serious training.

items.name: BF16/FP16 mixed-precision + AMP
items.whatItIs: Automatic mixed precision: compute matmuls in 16-bit while keeping master weights/reductions in FP32. BF16 shares FP32's 8-bit exponent so it needs no loss scaling.
items.whyForManwe: The portable default training precision across CUDA and MPS (both support BF16). Standardize on BF16 for detector training so the numerical path is reproducible on Apple and NVIDIA; reserve FP8/FP4 for NVIDIA-only runs.

items.name: torch.compile / TorchInductor (Triton)
items.whatItIs: PyTorch's graph compiler that fuses ops into Triton/CUDA kernels for large speedups on NVIDIA. On MPS the Inductor backend is immature (graph breaks, recompiles, NotImplementedError, CPU fallbacks).
items.whyForManwe: Use torch.compile on CUDA training/inference for real speedups; detect the MPS device and stay in eager mode there. Do not benchmark MPS-eager vs CUDA-compiled and call it a fair hardware comparison.

items.name: FlashAttention-3 / SDPA
items.whatItIs: IO-aware fused attention. FA-3 is native on NVIDIA Hopper; on MPS there is no native FlashAttention, only PyTorch SDPA without advanced features (variable-length, etc.).
items.whyForManwe: If crebain's detectors/temporal-fusion models use attention, expect a large CUDA advantage and reduced memory. On Apple Silicon, budget for slower attention via SDPA; keep sequence lengths modest for on-device paths.

items.name: DDP / FSDP2 + NCCL (multi-GPU)
items.whatItIs: Distributed Data Parallel and Fully Sharded Data Parallel v2 over NVIDIA NCCL/NVLink for multi-GPU/multi-node training, with per-module MixedPrecisionPolicy.
items.whyForManwe: The only practical way to scale detector training to large datasets/clusters — and it is NVIDIA-only in practice (MPS has no NCCL, only Gloo). Plan large training jobs on CUDA; Apple Silicon cannot serve this role.

items.name: FP8 / FP4 low precision (Transformer Engine, Blackwell)
items.whatItIs: Native 8-/4-bit training formats on recent NVIDIA (Hopper FP8, Blackwell NVFP4/MX), often requiring torch.compile and stochastic rounding for convergence. MPS has no native FP8/FP4 — it upcasts/emulates to BF16.
items.whyForManwe: A correctness-gap risk: a model 'trained' with emulated FP4 on a Mac can numerically diverge from real NVFP4 hardware. If you ever use FP8/FP4, validate on the actual NVIDIA GPU and keep BF16 as the portable baseline.

items.name: Burn + CubeCL (Rust multi-backend)
items.whatItIs: Next-gen Rust DL framework with a full training stack; CubeCL writes kernels once in Rust and runs on CUDA, ROCm, Metal, Vulkan and WebGPU, with kernel fusion on WGPU/CUDA.
items.whyForManwe: The strongest Rust candidate if manwe wants one codebase that both trains and runs kernels on NVIDIA and Apple GPUs. More training-capable than Candle; consider for a unified Rust train+deploy pipeline, accepting it is younger than PyTorch.

items.name: ExecuTorch
items.whatItIs: PyTorch's unified on-device runtime that lowers exported PyTorch programs to hardware delegates (CoreML/Metal on Apple, CUDA/others on NVIDIA), now a coremltools conversion source.
items.whyForManwe: An emerging single export pipeline from a PyTorch model to both Apple and NVIDIA on-device targets. Worth tracking as a future replacement for maintaining separate ONNX/CoreML/TensorRT exporters.

items.name: MLPerf Inference v5.1 methodology
items.whatItIs: Industry-standard cross-hardware benchmark; LoadGen issues fixed request patterns under latency/accuracy constraints. Convention: discard 10-20 warmup iters, time 100+ iters, lock clocks, report P50/P95/P99 + throughput, log full env.
items.whyForManwe: The template for making manwe's Apple-vs-NVIDIA numbers credible. Extend metal-yolo-tests to follow this protocol (device sync, percentiles not just mean FPS, pinned versions) and to also cover CUDA/TensorRT, not just the three Apple backends.

items.name: Palettization / 4-bit quantization (coremltools.optimize)
items.whatItIs: Core ML weight compression: pruning, quantization, and n-bit palettization (incl. mixed-bit picking 1/2/4/6/8-bit per layer, per-channel scale) targeting ANE-supported bit-widths.
items.whyForManwe: Shrinks crebain detectors for on-device Apple deployment and can improve ANE latency/power. Apply mixed-bit palettization to the .mlmodelc export while checking accuracy on drone/bird/aircraft classes.

items.name: MLX distributed training (JACCL, WWDC26)
items.whatItIs: Apple's collective-communication library running MLX collectives over RDMA on Thunderbolt 5 (~50-60 Gbps, sub-50us latency), enabling multi-Mac clusters (up to ~3x inference with four Macs).
items.whyForManwe: The first credible multi-device Apple-Silicon training/inference path. Useful for scaling Apple-side experimentation, but it closes the memory gap, not the bandwidth gap — a discrete NVIDIA GPU still wins on raw training throughput.

items.name: PYTORCH_ENABLE_MPS_FALLBACK + MPS operator-coverage tracking
items.whatItIs: Env flag routing unsupported MPS ops to CPU, plus PyTorch's living operator-coverage matrix (issue #141287). Some ops (e.g. certain Conv1d cases) still fail even with fallback; older builds had silent addcmul_/addcdiv_ and RNG bugs.
items.whyForManwe: Essential MPS hygiene: pin PyTorch >=2.4, enable the fallback but log every fallback, and add a CI assertion that the detector forward/backward produces no NaN and no silent CPU offload before trusting any Apple-Silicon training run.

items.name: Ultralytics export pipeline (ONNX/TensorRT/CoreML)
items.whatItIs: YOLO training + one-line export to TorchScript, ONNX, OpenVINO, TensorRT, and CoreML, with INT8 support across TensorRT/ONNX/CoreML.
items.whyForManwe: manwe's detectors are YOLO-family; this is the fastest route to produce every crebain export format from one training run. Use device='cuda' for training, then export to TensorRT (NVIDIA) and CoreML (Apple); use MPS mainly for local eval.

keyClaims.claim: On identical transformer inference an NVIDIA A10 beats Apple Silicon: BERT-base averaged ~23.5ms on CUDA vs ~38ms on M2-Max MLX and ~179ms on M1 MLX (XLM-RoBERTa 16ms vs 27ms vs 142ms); the authors conclude Apple Silicon is viable for research/prototyping but full production workloads remain impractical on consumer devices.

MPS training materially lags CUDA in 2025-2026: no native FP8/FP4 (emulated by upcasting to BF16), no float64, only the Gloo backend (no NCCL/NVLink) so large multi-GPU training is impractical, immature torch.compile/Inductor that falls back to CPU, and no native FlashAttention (SDPA only).
